instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: HISTORY: Hypotension, tender right lower quadrant, elevated white count, hematocrit drop. TECHNIQUE: Axial images of the abdomen and pelvis were obtained without IV contrast due to elevated creatinine. CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITHOUT IV CONTRAST: There are small bilateral pleural effusions and bibasilar dependent atelectases. There are coronary artery calcifications, median sternotomy and a prosthetic valve. There is a large hiatal hernia. The aorta and major branches are heavily calcified. There is a probable calcified mural thrombus in the region of the SMA. There is a low attenuation lesion in the posterior aspect of the right lobe of the liver which is poorly defined in this noncontrast study and probably represents the hemangioma seen on prior CT colonoscopy. Again seen are gallstones. The pancreas is atrophic with fatty infiltration. The spleen is not visualized. The left kidney is atrophic, there is a 1.5 cm exophytic lesion with Hounsfield units of approximately 40, unchanged to prior exam and probably represents a cyst with proteinaceous contents. There is a tiny high attenuation lesion in the upper pole of the left kidney consistent with a hemorrhagic cyst. The right kidney is unremarkable without evidence of stones or hydronephrosis. The adrenals are normal. There is an NG tube with the tip in the antrum. Loops of small bowel are not dilated. There is a large heterogeneous collection which tracks along the right posterior pararenal space and involving the right psoas muscle. There is minimal stranding in the righ perinephric space, there is a small amount of fluid in the perihepatic region. The largest portion of this collection measures 11 cm and it is located superior to the right iliacus muscle. There are multiple clips in the right para-aortic region. CT OF THE PELVIS WITHOUT IV CONTRAST: There are extensive artifacts from bilateral hip replacements which limit the evaluation of the pelvis. There is a Foley catheter within the bladder. There is slight displacement of the bladder towards the left by the right retroperitoneal collection. There are extensive degenerative changes in the spine. IMPRESSION: 1. Large right retroperitoneal hemorrhage extending from the pelvis to the right posterior pararenal space and psoas muscle. There is a small amount of perihepatic fluid. Findings were discussed with medical team.

input: INDICATION: 19-year-old female status post MVA. TECHNIQUE: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. CONTRAST: 150 cc of Optiray IV due to patient trauma. FINDINGS CHEST CT: There is a right pneumothorax. Pulmonary contusions are identified in the anterior and posterior segments of the right upper lobe, as well as the lateral segment of the medial lobe and, likely, the lateral basal segment of the right lower lobe. There is bibasilar atelectasis. The patient is intubated and there is a nasogastric tube in the esophagus. The tracheobronchial tree is grossly patent. There is a non-displaced fracture of the anterior aspect of the first rib on the right. There is subcutaneous emphysema on the right. There is no mediastinal hematoma. The aorta and pulmonary arteries are unremarkable. There is no pericardial effusion. CT ABDOMEN: There is a liver contusion involving the right lobe with linear extension to the capsule, consistent with laceration. The hypodensity does not extend into the porta hepatis. There is a small subcapsular hematoma. Fluid is also seen surrounding the right lobe of the liver, extending down both paracolic gutters and into the pelvis. The spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. The abdominal and pelvic osseous structures are intact. CT PELVIS: There is free fluid in the pelvis. The bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable. There is a Foley catheter within the bladder. minimal air in the bladder is identified, likely from catheterization procedure. CONCLUSION: 1. Right pneumothorax. No evidence of tension. Subcutaneous emphysema is also identified on the right. There is a non-displaced rib fracture of the first rib anteriorly on the right. 2. There is no mediastinal hematoma and there is no evidence of injury to the mediastinal great vessels. 3. Right pulmonary contusions. 4. Bibasilar atelectasis. 5. Liver contusion and laceration with subcapsular hematoma. There is free fluid in the abdomen and pelvis.

input: HISTORY: 30-year-old man with hepatocellular carcinoma, please do restaging CT, rule out metastasis. TECHNIQUE: MDCT images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis without and with IV contrast in multiple phases were obtained. Coronal and sagittal reconstructions were included in this study. Comparison is made to a prior CT torso dated ___. CT CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST: Interval development of innumerable pulmonary nodules throughout the lungs bilaterally. These pulmonary nodules are most numerous in the bilateral lower lobes (for example, images 24, 27, 29, 31, 32 and 34-37, series 4). There has been no significant interval change in the burden of pulmonary embolus (for example, images 26-29, series 4). The previously seen segmental infarct in the right lower lobe, peribronchail changes in the right upper lobe and patchy opacities in the right lower lobe have resolved, the latter likely represented inflammatory change. Visualized portion of the thyroid gland is unremarkable. Heart and great vessels appear normal. No evidence of pericardial or pleural effusion. Central airways are patent. No evidence of mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. CT ABDOMEN WITHOUT AND WITH IV CONTRAST: The patient is now status post partial right hepatectomy. Note is made of a large heterogeneous arterially enhancing mass protruding from the liver margin and involving the entire inferior aspect of this operative bed (measuring approximately 8.1 cm x 6.7 cm, image 45, series 4), which is highly concerning for residual hepatocellular carcinoma. There is a hypoattenuating region which extends from the surgical bed in the right lobe of the liver into the midddle hepatic vein and into the infracardiac IVC (images 7 through 24, series 3 A). This finding is consistent with IVC thrombus. Density measurements demonstrate enhancement in the thrombus from 46 to 82 ___ consistent with tumor thrombus. A gallstone is noted in the gallbladder. Pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. Small and large bowel appear normal. Aorta is normal in course and caliber. No evidence of free fluid or free air in the abdomen or pelvis. Note is made of subcentimeter mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymph nodes. CT PELVIS WITHOUT AND WITH IV CONTRAST: Bladder is distended and appears normal. Prostate gland is unremarkable. No evidence of pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. BONE WINDOWS: Osseous structures are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. Interval development of innumerable pulmonary nodules particularly in the lower lobes which are highly concerning for pulmonary metastases. No significant change in the pulmonary embolus burden. 2. Previously seen abnormalities in the right lung have resolved as described above. 2. There is a large soft tissue mass at the inferior aspect of the partial right hepatectomy bed (approximately 8.1 cm x 6.7 cm) which is consistent with residual hepatocellular carcinoma. 3. New tumor thrombus extending from the middle hepatic into the infracardiac IVC.

related: surgical bed in right lobe of liver (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: CT OF THE ABDOMEN: Mild bibasilar dependent atelectasis is noted. No pleural effusion is seen. Heart size is normal without pericardial effusion. Small hiatal hernia is noted. There are two moderate sized ventral hernias. A closed loop small bowel obstruction is seen within the inferior ventral hernia sac with two transition points, best demonstrated on the coronal view (601 b: 20), at the neck of the hernia sac. The closed loop of bowel demonstrates fecalization of contents and bowel wall enhancement, though, mild bowel wall edema is present, worrisome for ischemia (2:70). Small amount of free fluid is also seen within the hernia sac. The distal small bowel loops are decompressed. Proximal to the closed loop obstruction, the small bowel is dilated compatible with upstream obstruction. The superior ventral hernia sac contains a short segment of small bowel loop which is dilated up to 4 cm (602 b: 39). Liver demonstrates decreased attenuation, compatible with fatty deposition. No discrete hepatic lesion is identified. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. Hepatic vasculature is patent. A subcentimeter hypodensity involving spleen (2:30) was not seen on the prior exam and may represent a tiny hemangioma. Pancreas enhances homogeneously without ductal dilatation or peripancreatic fluid collection. Adrenal glands are unremarkable. Kidneys enhance and excrete contrast symmetrically without evidence of hydronephrosis or renal masses. Multiple focal hypodensities involving the left kidney are noted, many of which are too small to characterize and likely represent cysts. The largest hypodense lesion arising from the interpolar region measures 1 x 1.3 cm (2:46), it is partially exophytic and measures 40 Hounsfield units in attenuation, stable. Intra-abdominal aorta and IVC are normal in caliber. No pathologically enlarged mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymph nodes are seen. There is no free air within the abdomen. CT OF THE PELVIS: Bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable. The patient is status post total colectomy with suture line is noted at the site of ileoanal anastomosis (2:79). The uterus appears lobulated, with a likely posterior fibroid noted. There is no free air within the pelvis. No pathologically enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes are seen. OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion is seen. Multilevel degenerative joint changes of the lower lumbar spine are noted. IMPRESSION: 1. Closed loop small bowel obstruction in the inferior ventral hernia sac. Small amount of free fluid is seen within the hernia sac at the site of obstruction. There is no loss of bowel wall enhancement; however obstructed bowel loop demonstrates mild mural edema, concerning for ischemia. 2. Proximal to the closed loop obstruction there is an upstream small bowel obstruction, with a dilated loop of small bowel in the superior ventral hernia sac. 3. Stable appearance of left renal cysts. 4. Fatty deposition of the liver. 5. Small hiatal hernia.

related: short segment of small bowel loop (location:present)

input: INDICATION: 86-year-old female status post aortic valvuloplasty with new hematocrit drop, to evaluate for retroperitoneal bleed. COMPARISON: None. TECHNIQUE: Multidetector non-enhanced axial CT images of the abdomen and pelvis are obtained with multiplanar reformatted images. CT ABDOMEN WITHOUT CONTRAST: The lung bases demonstrate bibasilar atelectasis. There is no evidence for pericardial or pleural effusion. Atherosclerotic coronary artery and aortic valve calcifications are partially imaged. There is a moderately large hiatal hernia contained in the stomach. Limited non-contrast evaluation of the liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands is normal. Non-contrast evaluation of the kidneys demonstrates exophytic left renal cysts, the largest at the left upper pole measuring 6.2 x 6.8 mm. Intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel are of normal caliber and there is no pneumoperitoneum. Atherosclerotic calcifications involve the abdominal aorta and its branches though the abdominal aorta is of normal caliber. No pathologically enlarged mesenteric or retroperitoneal adenopathy is defined. CT PELVIS WITHOUT CONTRAST: There is extensive sigmoid diverticulosis without evidence for acute diverticulitis. The Foley contains a bladder and is collapsed. The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable. Right inguinal hemorrhagic stranding is associated with small subcutaneous and perivascular hematomas individually measuring up to 2-cm. This process extends inferiorly along the common femoral artery and vein, incompletely imaged off the caudal field of view. Mild extension in the pelvis along the external iliac vessels is also noted. Hemorrhagic expansion of the right pectineus muscle is associated. Bone windows reveal no worrisome osteolytic or osteosclerotic lesions. Changes related to prior left iliac bone harvesting are observed. There is extensive distal thoracolumbar spondylosis and exaggeration of lumbar lordosis with multilevel facet arthropathy. Multilevel compression deformities involve L 1 and a sclerotic T 11. IMPRESSION: 1. No retroperitoneal hematoma. Right inguinal hemorrhagic stranding and small hematomas, as described. 2. Sigmoid diverticulosis without acute diverticulitis. 3. Severe lumbar spondylosis with multilevel vertebral compression deformities, associated with a sclerotic vertebral body at T 11, a nonspecific finding. No history of malignancy is known. If outside priors are available, these could be compared or MRI could be pursued for further evaluation, as indicated.

input: INDICATION: Pedestrian struck. No comparison studies available. TECHNIQUE: MDCT-acquired 5-mm axial images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were obtained following the uneventful administration of 130 mL of Optiray intravenous contrast. Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed at 5-mm slice thickness. CHEST: An endotracheal tube terminates 3 cm above the carina. A transesophageal catheter terminates within the stomach. There is no axillary or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. The heart size is normal, and there is no pericardial effusion. The great vessels are patent and normal in caliber. No pulmonary embolus is detected to the segmental levels. A small subpleural pneumatocele at the left apex is likely secondary to blunt trauma (2:15). Moderate dependent atelectasis is seen bilaterally. There are no nodules or masses. ABDOMEN: The liver, gallbladder, adrenal glands, kidneys, stomach, and intra-abdominal loops of small and large bowel are within normal limits. Small grade I laceration of the spleen is present (2:49), resulting in a small to moderate amount of hemoperitoneum. There is no free air. There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. PELVIS: The rectum, sigmoid colon, intrapelvic loops of small and large bowel, bladder, uterus, and adnexa are within normal limits. There is no intrapelvic lymphadenopathy. Stranding overlying the left groin is likely related to attempted line placement. OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: An acute fracture of the mid left clavicle is present (2:3). Non-displaced fracture of the right first rib (2:5) is better visualized on the CT cervical spine examination performed on the same day. There are equivocal nondisplaced left transverse process fractures at L 4 and L 5. No other fractures are present. No concerning blastic or lytic lesions are identified. Sclerosis at the anterior and lateral aspect of the sacroiliac joints bilaterally (2:98) is compatible with osteitis condensans ilii. IMPRESSION: 1. Grade 1 splenic laceration, with a small to moderate volume hemoperitoneum. 2. Non-displaced right first rib fracture. 3. Acute left mid clavicular fracture. 4. Equivocal nondisplaced left transverse process tip fractures at L 4 and L 5.

input: FINDINGS: Superficial and deep femoral arteries are patent bilaterally. There is mild-to-moderate vessel disease with calcification in the SFA bilaterally, worst in the adductor canal. In the left lower extremity, the anterior tibial artery is seen opacified for a short distance from the tibioperoneal trunk and disappears before reaching the interosseous membrane. There is no reconstitution of the anterior tibial distally. The left posterior tibial and peroneal artery are patent through to the left foot. Within the right popliteal artery, near the lateral femoral condyle, is a non-obstructing thrombus which correlates with ultrasound findings from ___. The right anterior tibial artery is clearly seen originating from the tibioperoneal trunk and appears patent to the right foot, but appears moderately diseased and calcified. No posterior tibial opacification is observed. The peroneal artery is seen to opacify weakly for a short distance and then disappears. This vessel is reconstituted more distally in the lower calf. A left common iliac vein ___ is seen in position with no obvious complications; however, it is visualized on early phase and is not seen to opacify. The visualized small bowel and colon are normal in course and caliber. The rectum, prostate, bladder, and seminal vesicles are unremarkable. There is no free fluid or air within the abdomen or pelvis. No retroperitoneal, inguinal, or pelvic lymphadenopathy is noted. BONE WINDOW: Severe degenerative changes of the lumbar spine are noted. There are no lytic or blastic lesions suspicious for malignancy. IMPRESSION: 1. Stenosis in the right SFA in the adductor canal and in the right popliteal artery. The right anterior tibial artery appears patent. The right peroneal artery opacifies only a short distance from its origin but reconstitutes in the lower calf. A right posterior tibial artery cannot be seen. 2. Stenosis in the left SFA with 2-vessel runoff. The left anterior tibial artery opacifies for only a short segment and disappears before reaching the interosseous membrane. The left posterior tibial and peroneal arteries are seen to opacify from its origin through to the left foot. 3. Left common iliac vein ___

input: INDICATION: Recent sigmoidectomy for perforated colon. Evaluate for fluid collection. COMPARISON: CT torso, ___. TECHNIQUE: Oral contrast was administered and a Multidetector scanner used to obtain contiguous axial images from the lung bases to the pubic symphysis. No IV contrast was administered. Images were reconfigured and reformatted into coronal and sagittal planes. CT ABDOMEN WITHOUT IV CONTRAST: There is atelectasis and consolidation at both lung bases, with pleural effusions, left greater than right. The liver, pancreas, right adrenal, and small bowel are within normal limits; the left adrenal is not visualized. The left kidney is congenitally absent; the right kidney has a ureteral stent. The spleen is surgically absent, with clips in the surgical bed. The stomach and duodenum are dilated, with a 6-cm portion of proximal small bowel with a thickened wall; contrast is seen beyond it, though most of the bowel is collapsed. Additionally, the previously seen hematoma in the left upper quadrant appears adjacent to and surrounding this area of small bowel thickening, which itself may represent an area of hematoma, infection, or inflammation. There is free fluid within the intraperitoneal space and fluid invaginating between the intraperitoneal organs. CT OF THE PELVIS WITHOUT IV CONTRAST: There is a moderate amount of pelvic fluid. There is air within the bladder, as well as a Foley catheter. There is a stoma; there is retained contrast within the rectum and surgical clips at its superior aspect; all of which indicate partial colectomy. The distal portion of the right ureteral stent can be seen within the bladder. The prostate appears normal. The bowel loops are collapsed. Osseous structures are remarkable for severe degenerative changes of the lumbar spine. Subcutaneous tissues show marked edema. Coronal and sagittal images show the area of small bowel thickening particularly well, and were essential in establishing the diagnosis. IMPRESSION: 1. Partial small bowel obstruction with a thickened portion of the small bowel invested by / adjacent to preexisting hematoma, which may represent hematoma, infection, or inflammation. 2. Anasarca, with free fluid within the abdomen. 3. Congenitally absent left kidney; right kidney with ureteral stent. 4. Surgically absent spleen. 5. Bilateral atelectasis / consolidation and pleural effusions. 6. Status post partial colectomy. These findings were discussed with the Surgical intern on ___, at 12:00.

input: FINDINGS: CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST: The lungs appear similar to chest CT on ___ with resolved pleural effusions and some residual right lower lobe atelectasis versus scarring. The visualized heart and pericardium are unremarkable. At the site of the previous liver laceration in segment VII / VIII, there is some hypodense, heterogeneous material that either represents Surgicel or residual fluid. The remaining liver is unremarkable. The gallbladder is unremarkable. There is no intra-or extra-hepatic biliary dilatation. The pancreas, spleen, kidneys, and adrenal glands are unremarkable. The portal vein is patent. There is no periportal, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. The intraabdominal vasculature is unremarkable. The stomach, duodenum, and small bowel are unremarkable. The colon is within normal limits. There is no free air. There are no abdominal wall hernias. There is a phlegmonous collection in the anterior abdomen at the wound site measuring 2.8 x 1.4 x 7.3 cm. There is irregular peripheral and internal enhancement, with inflammatory stranding and no organized drainable collections. This inflammation appears to invade the rectus abdominis muscles, best seen on series 602 b, image 34. However, there is no extension inferior to the rectus abdominis muscle. There is no intraabdominal extension. There is no evidence of enterocutaneous fistula. CT OF THE PELVIS WITH CONTRAST: The uterus, adnexa, and rectum are unremarkable. The bladder and terminal ureters are unremarkable. There is no pelvic wall or inguinal lymphadenopathy. There is no pelvic free fluid. OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: There are bilateral old healing fractures of the pubic rami. IMPRESSION: 1. Phlegmonous collection at the anterior abdominal wound site with stranding. No drainable collections. The collection infiltrates the adjacent rectus abdominis; however, it does not infiltrate into the abdominal cavity. There is no enterocutaneous fistula. 2. Residual hypodense and heterogeneous area in segment VII / VIII of the liver either represents residual changes / fluid at the site of prior laceration or Surgicel. 3. Resolved pleural effusions. Residual right lower lobe atelectasis versus scarring.

input: (REVISED) HISTORY: Myelodysplastic syndrome. History of mastectomy for breast CA. Recent portal vein thrombosis, now status post splenectomy with? of extravasation of oral contrast on outside CT. Fever. TECHNIQUE: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis. CONTRAST: 100 cc Optiray. COMPARISON: ___ from ___ AND ___ ___ CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST: There is a 7.5 mm focal pleural-based area of opacity at the right lung base anteriorly, which likely represents atelectasis. Lung bases are otherwise clear and there are no effusions. There is high attenuation surgical material in the splenectomy bed. The splenic vein is noted to be thrombosed and expanded. The IMV is patent, though the origin of the portal vein is partially thrombosed. The left and right portal veins and the distal portion of the main portal vein is completely patent. The branch to segment three and the posterior branch on the right. In the splenectomy bed there are multiple tiny rounded areas of low attenuation in association with the sutures which is not significantly changed since the previous study. This likely represents thrombus in discrete branches of the splenic vein vs. packing material. There is also a small amount of ascites about the splenectomy bed and anterior to the liver. There is a multiseptated low attenuation lesion replacing the majority of Segment 3 of the liver. There are no additional liver lesions identified. The gallbladder and adrenal gland on the right are unremarkable. The left kidney is in the pelvis. The right kidney contains a tiny hypodense cortical lesion laterally, which is too small to accurately characterize. There is no adenopathy identified. The bowel is unremarkable. There is nodularity in the omentum in the left upper quadrant which is less prominent in the interval. This may represent resolving inflammatory phenomena or possibly reduction in size of unusual venous collaterals. CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST: There is no free intraperitoneal air. The urinary bladder is unremarkable. There are no suspicious osseous lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. Multiseptate left hepatic lobe liver lesion distorting the liver contour. Given the history of fever and the appearance of this lesion, a liver abscess is possible. Sequelae of ischemic injury related to bile duct injury is another possibility. Given the elevated INR, percutaneous sampling would not be prudent at this time but this lesion is amenable to FNA when coagulation parameters corrected. 2. Enlarged thrombosed splenic vein with partial thrombosis of portal vein and branches. IMV is patent--much improved over prior ___ scan. 3. Small amount of ascites. 4. High attenuation material in splenic bed represents surgical clips rather than extravasated contrast.

related: distal portion of main portal vein (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: When compared to the prior MRI examination, again noted are multiple T 2 / FLAIR foci throughout the brain, however, the majority of them have had an interval decrease in FLAIR signal, a few of them have almost resolved. Their difference in FLAIR signal particularly in the focus along the splenium of the corpus callosum suggests different acuities with some foci more recent than others. A few of them demonstrate more intense high signal in the gradient echo sequence with associated high signal in the T 1-weighted images consistent with late subacute blood products. The area of concern in the recent CT scan correlates to one of the abnormal foci of high FLAIR / T 2 signal in the left cerebellum consistent with evolving encephalomalacia. This focus also exhibits decreased FLAIR intensity when directly compared to the prior examination. There is no evidence of new lesions. There is no evidence of abnormal signal in the ADC map or abnormal enhancement. The paranasal sinuses are well aerated. There is partial fluid within both mastoid air cells, mildly improved since the prior examination of ___. IMPRESSION: 1. Evolution of multiple abnormal FLAIR foci, in keeping with infarcts, throughout the brain parenchyma with some of them demonstrating more apparent hemorrhagic components. Different degrees of decreased FLAIR intensity involving some of the multiple infarcts. No evidence of acute infarct. 2. The area of concern corresponds to expected evolution of a focal infarct within the left cerebellum. No evidence of abnormal enhancement.

input: FINDINGS: There are new areas of slow diffusion in left frontal and parietal lobes involving the precentral gyrus suggestive of acute infarcts. Areas of slow diffusion are noted in left corona radiata, left thalamus, left parieto-occipital regions and splenium of corpus callosum which are unchanged since the prior study. Areas of gliosis with FLAIR and T 2 hyperintensities are noted in bilateral occipital lobes which are sequelae of subacute / chronic infarcts (left greater than right). There is no evidence of hemorrhagic transformation of infarcts. There is prominence of the cortical sulci, ventricular system and extra-axial CSF spaces suggestive of generalized cerebral atrophy. Mucosal thickening is noted in the sphenoid sinus and bilateral ethmoid air cells. Orbits are unremarkable. There is partial opacification of bilateral mastoid air cells. IMPRESSION: 1. New acute infarcts in left frontal and parietal lobes. 2. Subacute infarcts in splenium of corpus callosum, left thalamus and left parietal and frontal white matter. 3. Sequelae of subacute / chronic infarcts in bilateral occipital lobes. These findings were communicated to Dr. ___ ___ by Dr ___ via telephone at 10:30 AM on ___

input: FINDINGS: In the left lentiform nucleus, corresponding to the area of the previously noted large hemorrhage, there is restricted diffusion, which presumably represents the remnant of the prior hemorrhage. This area roughly conforms to the hypodensity noted on the more recent head CT scan. A larger area of edema surrounds this hemorrhage, and there is mild mass effect upon the left lateral ventricle. There is also a resultant few millimeters rightward shift of the septum pellucidum. There is extensive white matter T 2 hyperintensity within both cerebral hemispheres, as well as within the pons. No other overt abnormality is seen, although it is to be noted that the examination is of very limited quality due to gross patient motion. CONCLUSION: Abnormalities likely reflecting prior hemorrhage. Motion degraded study. See above report for additional details.

input: FINDINGS: Since the prior MRI study, there is no significant change in the left lateral ventricular subependymoma measuring 1.9 x 0.9 x 0.7 cm, best appreciated in series 9, image 15. There is unchanged left frontal encephalomalacia with postoperative changes and persistent dilatation of the left frontal ___ of the lateral ventricle. There is mild decrease in the enhancement adjacent to the surgical cavity reflecting post-operative changes. The patient is status post left frontal craniotomy with the surgical tract visualized. There is minimal hyperintense signal abnormality adjacent to the surgical bed on the FLAIR sequence and is unchanged. There are no new lesions identified in the brain. There is no evidence of midline shift or mass effect. The posterior fossa structures appear unremarkable. The major vascular flow voids are well preserved. There is minimal mucosal thickening of the ethmoidal sinuses visualized bilaterally. The visualized orbits appear normal. IMPRESSION: 1. Since prior MRI study, there is no significant change in the left ventricle subependymoma with decreased enhancment in the periphery of the surgical bed and persistent dilation of the left lateral ventricle. 2. No new lesions identified in the brain. Recommend continued followup with MRI studies.

related: left frontal portion of lateral ventricle (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: There are postoperative changes seen new since the previous MRI examination with resection of left frontal lobe mass. There is persistent mass effect on the left lateral ventricle. The inner component of the mass demonstrates similar appearances with blood products seen in the peripheral lateral component. However, on the diffusion images there is now new restricted diffusion involving the inner portion of this tumor. This could be secondary to developing infarction within the residual mass. Alternatively this could be secondary to postoperative change. Blood products are seen within the mass. There is no residual enhancement identified. There is mild midline shift to the right side as before. There is no hydrocephalus seen. Small extra-axial collection in the left frontoparietal region appears postoperative in nature. IMPRESSION: Status post resection of left frontal mass with blood products seen in the lateral portion of the mass where there has been resection. The medial and the anterior portion of the mass appears unchanged on T 2-weighted images but demonstrate restricted diffusion on the diffusion imaging which could be secondary to developing infarction within the residual mass or due to postoperative change. There remains mass effect on the left lateral ventricle with mild midline shift to the right side. No hydrocephalus. No acute infarct in the other part of the brain.

input: FINDINGS: This markedly motion-limited study is compared with the recent, complete contrast-enhanced MR examination dated ___. The enhancing posterior right parietal lobe intra-axial mass is redemonstrated. The mass again appears to demonstrate thick rind, as well as patchy central, enhancement with relatively non-enhancing, ___ foci, likely representing focal cystic necrosis. Change in the previously very slight associated vasogenic edema, mass effect and midline shift is quite difficult to assess on this study, but appears worse, with more marked effacement of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle. There is no definite associated hemorrhage (no pre-contrast sequence is provided). Note that on the previous exam the mass demonstrates apparent decreased diffusion, implying high cellular density; such a phenomenon may be seen in primary CNS lymphoma and, occasionally, in metastatic disease, particularly small cell carcinoma of the lung. IMPRESSION: Very limited examination, re-demonstrating the predominantly enhancing posterior right parietal mass with apparently worse mass effect and effacement of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle. The imaging findings on the previous examination are suggestive of a hypercellular intra-axial neoplasm, possibly metastasis from apparently known carcinoma of the lung.

input: FINDINGS: A region of increased FLAIR / T 2 signal within the left thalamus extending into the left cerebral peduncle is again demonstrated. On the FLAIR and T 2-weighted sequences, this appears similar to the prior examinations. However, the lesion now enhances which it did not do in ___. More remote examinations do reveal enhancement in this area such as on ___. No new lesions are identified. There is no evidence of infarct or new mass. The ventricles, cisterns and sulci are otherwise similar to prior with mild, symmetric prominence. Intracranial flow voids are preserved. Extracranial soft tissue and osseous structures are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: Left thalamic / cerebral peduncle lesion now showing enhancement, otherwise similar to prior. The interval development of enhancement may represent interval immunologic response since the patient is now back on antiretroviral therapy. Interval worsening of cerebral toxoplasmosis could have a similar appearance, however, and continued followup is suggested.

input: PROCEDURE: MRI of the brain without contrast. INDICATION: New left-sided intraventricular hemorrhage. Rule out underlying AVM or mass. TECHNIQUE: Attempt at MRI brain study was terminated early due to difficulty controlling patient motion. Only T 1 sagittal images were acquired. MRI OF THE BRAIN WITHOUT CONTRAST: Limited study was performed. Only T 1 sagittal weighted images were acquired. These images are markedly degraded by patient motion. As seen on recent CT study of the brain on ___, there is heterogeneous increased signal intensity within the occipital and temporal horns of the left lateral ventricle consistent with blood products. The ventricles are prominent in size, as noted previously. No gross, obvious T 1 signal abnormalities are noted within the brain parenchyma. IMPRESSION: Early termination of study-only T 1 sagittal images were obtained. Full repeat MRI of the brain is recommended when patient's ability to cooperate is optimized.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: Post-surgical changes are again noted in the region of the right posterior fossa, status post suboccipital craniotomy. Both the post-surgical changes and underlying brain were better evaluated on the prior examination done two days prior. MR ___ ___ absence of flow-related enhancement in the distal right transverse sinus, extending into the right sigmoid sinus, with absence of flow as well in the right internal jugular vein. The findings are most consistent with a thrombus, well appreciated on the prior examination. There may be small residual flow within the right internal jugular vein versus a second accessory vein. IMPRESSION: Sinus thrombosis of the distal right transverse sinus, extending into the right sigmoid sinus and right internal jugular vein. The findings were conveyed to Dr. ___ ___ at the time of initial interpretation at 11:20 a. m.

input: FINDINGS: PRELIMINARY REPORT: No venous sinus thrombosis or infarct. Sinus disease. Fluid in nasopharynx and mastoids. ___, ___: FINDINGS: MRI OF THE HEAD: There are no areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction. No areas of T 2 susceptibility are noted in the brain parenchyma. The ventricles and extra-axial CSF spaces are unremarkable. There is increased signal in the ethmoid, maxillary, sphenoid and the frontal sinuses related to mucosal thickening and fluid. The major vascular arterial flow voids are noted. MRV OF THE HEAD: The major dural venous sinuses appear patent. Decreased signal intensity in the posterior part of the superior sagittal sinus likely relates to slow flow given the lack of hyperintensity on the sagittal T 1-weighted sequence. IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of acute infarction. 2. Patent major dural venous sinuses. Decreased signal in the posterior part of the superior sagittal sinus, including the torcular herophili and the right sigmoid sinus, likely relates to slow flow rather than thrombosis given the lack of increased signal on the sagittal T 1-weighted sequences. If there is continued concern based on the clinical presentation, CTA and CTV, if serum creatinine level is appropriate or a followup MR venogram can be considered.

input: INDICATION: 77-year-old female, status post cardiac surgery with sternal fluid collection. Evaluate. COMPARISON: Chest x-rays dating back to _ _ _. TECHNIQUE: MDCT imaging of the chest was performed following the administration of 75 cc of intravenous Optiray. Coronal reformatted images were obtained. CT CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: The soft tissue of the sternotomy is widely separated from the sternal notch extending inferiorly for approximately 6.6 cm. Below the xiphoid, a second skin defect measures approximately 5 cm long with a subcutaneous, air-containing pocket extending inferiorly. There is no presternal fluid collection and adjacent subcutaneous fat stranding is consistent with recent surgery. The split sternum is not yet fused. There is no bone erosion to suggest osteomyelitis. At the level of the xiphoid process, a 3.5 x 1.8 cm, homogenous retrosternal soft tissue lesion, could be residual hematoma or phlegmon but does not have the characteristics of an abscess at this time. Mild stranding in the retrosternal soft tissues at the level of the aortic root is consistent with recent AVR as are small mediastinal fluid collections. There is no appreciable pericardial effusion or fluid collection near the prosthesis. Atherosclerotic calcifications line the thoracic aorta and right coronary artery. There are no lung nodules or any consolidation. A paraseptal, right lower lobe bulla with a slightly thickened wall is likely related to adjacent spinal DJD. There is no pneumothorax. There is a small volume of pleural fluid. Imaging of the upper abdomen is not sufficient for diagnosis but demonstrates cholecystectomy clips and a small / moderate hiatal hernia. IMPRESSION: 1. Open sternal wound, without presternal or retrosternal fluid collection or any evidence of osteomyelitis, continuous with large, blind-ending, infraxiphoid sinus tract. 2. Small retrosternal hematoma or phlegmon should be followed if patient develops signs of deep infection. 3. Minimal juxtacardiac and mediastinal fluid, consistent with recent surgery. 4. Small / moderate hiatal hernia.

input: FINDINGS: The pulmonary arteries are well opacified showing no evidence of PE. The aorta is unremarkable, showing no evidence of dissection or aneurysmal dilatation. The heart size is normal. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. In the left upper lobe, there is a focal area of peripheral airspace disease (3:30) and a solid nodule (3:45), which measures approximately 6.3 mm that contain a small converging bronchiole. In the left lung apex, there is a pleural parenchymal nodular thickening which measures 8.6 mm (3:9). No other nodules are seen. At the lung bases, there are areas of bibasilar small atelectasis. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. Tracheostomy tube is seen terminating in the thoracic inlet portion of the trachea distally, the trachea demonstrate mild collapse which might represent tracheomalacia. The right main bronchus and bronchus intermedius also show some evidence of bronchomalacia. A right internal jugular line is embedded in the right internal jugular vein and terminates in the right atrium.. There is calcification of the left coronary artery. In the abdomen / pelvic region, the liver is atrophic with irregularities of its surface consistent with cirrhosis. The patient is status post splenectomy from massive splenomegaly seen on the _ _ _ examination. The gallbladder is contracted, thickened in outline with pericholecystic edema. The kidneys and adrenals are unremarkable. The small and large bowel are normal in caliber. The patient is status post repair of ventral hernia with persistent drainage catheter embedded in the subcutaneous region. Minimal free fluid in the dependent portion of the pelvis are seen, right more than left. A Foley catheter is seen in the bladder, which is relatively empty. The prostate is mildly enlarged. The iliac veins are well opacified showing no evidence of DVT. IVC is unremarkable. The breast tissues are prominent in keeping with gynecomastia. The bony structures do not show any lesions suspicious for malignancy and / or infection, just degenerative changes mainly in the spine. IMPRESSION: 1. Negative examination for PE or DVT. 2. Negative examination for aortic dissection. 3. Peripheral focal area of airspace disease in the left upper lobe along with two nodules in the left upper lobe and left lung apex which could be a sequela of prior infection; however, a followup examination in four weeks by a chest CT scan examination is recommended. The examination might include a CT trachea protocol since there are elements of tracheobronchomalacia. 4. Left coronary artery calcification. 5. Cirrhotic liver, status post splenectomy / 6. Gallbladder wall thickening and wall edema likely due to the patient's underlying cirrhotic abnormality. 7. Free fluid in the pelvis. 8. Lines, tubes, and catheters are in a satisfactory location with the exception of the right IJ line which terminates in the right atrium and needs to be repositioned. This report has been placed on the radiology dashboard for it to be conveyed to the referring doctor.

input: INDICATION: Squamous cell lung cancer, status post left pulmonectomy _ _ _, with right lower lobe endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma _ _ _ treated with photodynamic therapy and recent bronchoscopy (_ _ _) demonstrating right lower lobe cavitary lesion with atypical squamous cells. Please evaluate for recurrent disease. TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT images were obtained through the chest with contrast in standard and lung algorithm. Coronal reformatted images were obtained. COMPARISON: Chest radiograph 8 / _ _ _. CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST: The patient is status post left pneumonectomy and the left main bronchus stump is unremarkable. Simple fluid fills the left hemithorax. Calcification of the thick pneumonectomy rind is marked. Left hemithorax volume loss and mediastinal shift are moderate. The right lung is hyperexpanded and shows moderate-to-severe apical predominant emphysema. The trachea and right main bronchus are patent. A 5.4 x 2.9-cm, thick but smooth-walled air-filled cavity is continuous with a broad ulceration of the bronchus intermedius. This cavity is located between the right pulmonary artery and thoracic spine at the junction of the bronchi to the right upper lobe and superior segment right lower lobe, but whether it is located in lung, pleural space or mediastinum is unclear. Adjacent to this cavity, 12 mm of soft tissue surrounding the right middle lobe bronchus is the only suggestion of local tumor recurrence. Localized fibrosis and traction bronchiectasis in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe is moderate. Heart size is normal. Atherosclerotic calcifications within the aortic annulus, arch and descending aorta are moderate. Mitral valve calcifications are mild. Anterior descending and left circumflex coronary artery atherosclerotic calcifications are moderate. There is no pericardial or right pleural effusion. No central pathologically enlarged lymph nodes by size criteria. Limited images through the upper abdomen show normal adrenal glands and unremarkable portions of the liver and spleen. There are no osseous findings of malignancy. Thoracotomy changes are seen of the left upper posterior ribs. IMPRESSION: 1. Cavitary lesion continuous with an ulcerated bronchus intermedius has non-aggressive appearing thickened wall with smooth margins, but a small focus of soft tissue surrounding the right middle lobe bronchus could be tumor. CT FDG PET-CT might be able to localize tumor, but discrimination from the inflammation of the large pocket may be problem _ _ _. 2. Focal fibrosis and traction bronchiectasis in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe may be sequelae to radiation therapy. 3. Status post left pneumonectomy with unremarkable left main bronchus stump. 4. Severe apical predominant emphysema.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: INDICATION: 50-year-old with hypotension, fever, and prostate biopsy two days ago with no palpable postop pulses. Evaluate for open grafts and fever source. COMPARISON: _ _ _. TECHNIQUE: Multidetector helical scanning of the abdomen, pelvis, and lower extremities was performed prior to and following the administration of IV Optiray contrast. Delayed images were also obtained through the abdomen, pelvis, and lower extremities. Further delayed images are obtained through the chest. There is moderate bibasilar atelectasis. No pleural effusion. The heart is enlarged, and there are multiple clips from prior CABG as well as median sternotomy wires. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber. The main pulmonary artery is enlarged measuring 3.8 cm, indicating underlying pulmonary arterial hypertension. Small mediastinal lymph nodes do meet CT criteria for pathologic enlargement. There is retrosternal soft tissue in the anterior mediastinum, which may represent rebound thymic tissue, however, should be watched closely. Endotracheal tube terminates within the mid trachea, and the bronchi are patent to the subsegmental level. CTA OF THE ABDOMEN: The liver, spleen, gallbladder, adrenal glands, and shrunken native kidneys are unremarkable. There is mildly prominent enhancement of the small bowel loops, likely due to arterial phase. No definite evidence of bowel wall thickening. The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber, however, has moderately extensive atherosclerotic calcification. There is a band-like focal stenosis at the origin of the celiac artery. There is also significant plaque within the proximal superior mesenteric artery which severely attenuates the vessel; however, the vessel remains patent. The _ _ _ is also patent. CT OF THE PELVIS: The appendix is normal. Transplanted kidney in the right lower quadrant enhances normally, and the renal artery which arises from the right external iliac artery is patent. Arterial outpouching from the left external iliac artery is due to a residual stump from prior pancreatic transplant. There is no pelvic free fluid or lymphadenopathy. Foley catheter and air are seen within the bladder. CTA OF THE LOWER EXTREMITIES: The very distal right common femoral artery is completely thrombosed. The right femoral popliteal graft arises from the thrombosed right common femoral artery, and there is no contrast seen within the graft. The right profunda opacifies via collaterals and maintains patency throughout the thigh. The right fem-_ _ _ graft is completely thrombosed extending into the native popliteal artery. Within the upper calf, there is minimal reconstitution of within the tibioperoneal trunk, likely from tiny collaterals from the profunda branches. Evaluation is limited due to extensive calcification within these vessels. On the left, there is a similar picture. The left common femoral artery is patent until just past the SFA / profunda bifurcation. The profunda is patent. The left superficial femoral artery is patent very proximally, however, thromboses as soon as it enters the fem anterior tibial graft. An additional abandoned fem-_ _ _ graft is noted, however, is completely thrombosed. The left profunda opacifies many deep perforating branches. The graft extends into the left anterior tibial artery, which is also thrombosed. There is minimal contrast opacification within the tibioperoneal trunk, likely due to profunda branches, also challenging to assess given the extent of vascular calcification. There is a moderate amount of subcutaneous edema involving the lower aspect of the left leg and extending into the left ankle. IMPRESSION: 1. Thrombosis of the right femoral-popliteal, and left femoral-anterior tibial grafts with minimal reconstitution at the level of the tibioperoneal trunks bilaterally via collaterals. The profunda arteries are patent bilaterally. 2. Unchanged plaque / thrombus within the SMA which attenuate the vessel, however, not significantly changed since _ _ _. No evidence of bowel ischemia. 3. Unremarkable right lower quadrant renal transplant. 4. Anterior mediastinal soft tissue which may represent thymic rebound. Close interval followup is recommended. 5. Bibasilar atelectasis.

related: very distal right common femoral artery (location:present)

input: INDICATION: Status post CABG, rule out sternal dehiscence and mediastinitis. COMPARISON: CT chest, _ _ _, not available at the time of dictation. TECHNIQUE: Contrast-enhanced axial CT imaging of the chest with coronal and sagittal reformations was reviewed. CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST: Please note that the study is limited secondary to poor contrast opacification of the heart and great vessels of the mediastinum. Contrast injection occurred via the left peripheral vein, and there is opacification of multiple collateral vessels circumventing left subclavian vein that itself demonstrated abrupt change in opacification. Left-sided transvenous pacers run through the left subclavian vein. These findings likely indicate left subclavian / brachiocephalic thrombosis. The patient is status post CABG. Multiple small nonpathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes are noted. The heart is enlarged. Patient is status post aortic valve replacement. There is pre-sternal and retrosternal stranding and inflammatory fluid consistent with mediastinitis, but no focal drainable fluid collection. Sternotomy wires are intact and there is no evidence for bony erosion along the sternotomy site to indicate osteomyelitis. There is a small pleural effusion. There is cardiomegaly. The lungs demonstrate nonspecific mosaic ground-glass opacification in the upper lobes. There is some linear atelectasis at the left base. Evaluation of the lungs is somewhat limited secondary to respiratory motion. Patient is status post anterior cervical spine fusion. Note of a left pacemaker with wires coursing their anticipated paths and a left humeral prosthesis. There is no evidence for sternal dehiscence or bony erosion to suggest osteomyelitis, and the bony structures are otherwise only remarkable for degenerative changes. IMPRESSION: 1. Inflammatory stranding around the sternum is consistent with mediastinitis, but there is no evidence for sternal dehiscence or other evidence for osteomyelitis. There are no drainable fluid collections. 2. Probable chronic left subclavian / brachiocephalic vein thrombosis about indwelling pacer wires. Ultrasound can be performed to confirm this suspicion. _ _ _ _ _ _ and I discussed these findings overnight.

input: INDICATIONS: Status post trauma with white-out of left lung. Question loculated effusion. COMPARISONS: Limited comparison is made to a CT of the thoracic spine, dated _ _ _ which depicted a portion of the lung parenchyma. TECHNIQUE: Axial CT images of the chest were obtained with intravenous contrast, and coronal reconstructions were also performed. CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST: The patient is status post CABG. The thyroid gland is relatively hypodense, which could be seen in a state of relatively low iodine content. Native coronary arteries appear calcified. The heart is mildly enlarged. There are multiple small subcentimeter mediastinal lymph nodes, which are most likely reactive. There is no pericardial effusion. However, there are small bilateral pleural effusions, greater on the right than left. The right-sided effusion is of intermediate density, up to 27 Hounsfield units, but is not signficantly changed. A small left effusion shows fluid-like density. Patchy bibasilar consolidative opacities are present, but markedly improved since the prior CT in the areas visualized on that study. A ground glass opacity in the right upper lobe which may have represented a focus of aspiration or contusion has mostly resolved. There is also reduction in interlobular septal thickening, compatible with resolution of pulmonary vascular congestion. The tranverse colon appears mildly distended with air up to 6.9 cm on the scout view. Otherwise limited views of upper abdominal organs are unremarkable. BONE WINDOWS: There are multiple right-sided rib fractures. There include non-displaced posterior third and fourth rib fractures. Minimally displaced fractures are present along both the posterior and lateral aspects of the fifth and sixth ribs. Mildly displaced segmental fractures are also present along the seventh and eighth ribs, with each fractured along both their posteromedial and lateral margins. A non-displaced lateral right ninth rib fracture is also visualized. No other fractures are visualized. IMPRESSION: 1. Multiple right-sided rib fractures, including segmental fractures involving the right fourth through eight ribs. 2. Small bilateral pleural effusions. The right-sided effusion is not significantly changed and shows intermediate density, which could reflect the presence of prior hemorrhage, probably related to rib fractures. 3. Improved bibasilar lung opacities, likely representin atelectasis. 4. Rapidly improved right apical ground-glass opacity probably reflecting resolving contusion or aspiration. 4. Hypoattenuating appearance of the thyroid, which could be seen in a state of low iodine content. 5. Mild colonic distention, with a transverse colon measuring up to 6.9 cm in diameter.

input: INDICATION: 54-year-old postop day 8 for right colectomy and end ileostomy with Hartmann procedure for perforation and hemorrhage post transverse colectomy for adenocarcinoma. TECHNIQUE: MDCT-acquired images were obtained through the chest, abdomen and pelvis after the uneventful administration of oral and 130 cc of IV Optiray contrast. Multiplanar reformats were reviewed. COMPARISON: _ _ _. CT CHEST: Moderate bilateral effusions are associated with relaxation atelectasis. There is no focal consolidation or definite pulmonary nodule. Heart size is normal. There is no pericardial effusion. Endotracheal tube terminates 5.5 cm above the carina. Nasogastric tube courses through the esophagus to terminate in the mid stomach. CT ABDOMEN: The liver, spleen, adrenals and pancreas are unremarkable. Sludge or contrast is noted in a nondistended galbladder. Post-colectomy and Hartmann pouch changes are noted. Moderate amount of ascites predominantly layers freeely without evidence of rim enhancement, however several areas layer nondependantly, raising concern for loculation. There is no gross pneumoperitoneum. Small locules of air adjacent to the splenic flexure anastomosis (2:77) and partially open anterior abdominal wall likely reflect postsurgical change. There is no fluid adjacent to a likley intact splenic flexure anastamosis. The kidneys excrete contrast and enhance symmetrically. The intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel demonstrate no evidence of pneumatosis. Bowel loops measure up to 3 cm in diameter without gross obstruction identified. Stranding along the subcutaneous tissues diffusely likely represents anasarca. CT PELVIS: Moderate pelvic free fluid is noted. Air in the bladder is likely secondary to Foley catheterization. The rectum and sigmoid are unremarkable. there is no pelvic or inguinal adenopathy. Bone windows demonstrate no evidence of lesion that is suspicious for metastasis or infection. IMPRESSION: 1. Post transverse colectomy with likely intact anastomotic site. Locules of air adjacent to the splenic flexure anastomosis likely represent post surgical change. 2. Areas of nondependant ascites raise the possibility of loculation. There is no rim enhancement to suggest abscess. 3. Anasarca and moderate bilateral pleural effusions. Findings were discussed with Dr. _ _ _ at the time of dictation.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: There is no suspicious sclerotic or lytic lesion. IMPRESSION: 1. Slightly techically limited study. Within that constraint there is no apparent pulmonary embolus. 2. Retained secretions seen in the right main stem bronchus as well as areas of consolidation the right lower lobe, suggestive of possible asprition. 3. Infrarenal IVC filter as above.
