Again seen is an ill-defined infiltrative mass within the pancreatic neck and body with marked atrophy and ductal dilatation in the pancreatic tail. The mass encases the celiac trunk and proximal celiac branches, proximal SMA and SMV, and adjacent portal vein segment. There is near complete occlusion of the SMV and portal vein by the mass with narrowing of the encased SMA and proximal celiac branches. Again seen is splenic vein thrombosis with peri-splenic and perigastric varices. There is focal narrowing the common bile duct at the level of the pancreatic head (3/354) likely secondary to disease involvement. No evidence of intrahepatic biliary dilatation is seen. The mass also involves the left adrenal gland (3/353), increased in severity from the prior study. In comparison to prior study, there is increased distention of the duodenum immediately proximal to the mass. There is abrupt narrowing of the duodenum as it passes posterior to the superior mesenteric vessels, increased in severity compared to the prior study. Findings are suspicious for direct invasion of the duodenal segment. Contrast passage into the distal small bowel is seen. The kidneys demonstrate symmetric enhancement without evidence of hydronephrosis. No evidence of radiodense renal stones. The small bowel and colon demonstrate no inflammatory changes or dilatation. There is unremarkable appearance of the colonic anastomosis side at the sigmoid colon. No free fluid in the abdomen or pelvis is seen. No evidence of discrete fluid collections to suggest abscess formation. No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions are seen. IMPRESSION: 1. ILL-DEFINED INFILTRATIVE MASS IN THE PANCREATIC NECK AND BODY WITH ENCASEMENT OF THE CELIAC TRUNK AND PROXIMAL BRANCHES AS WELL AS THE SMA, SMV, AND PORTAL VEIN. NEAR COMPLETE OCCLUSION OF THE ENCASED PORTIONS OF THE PORTAL VEIN AND SMV WITH NARROWING OF THE PROXIMAL CELIAC ARTERY BRANCHES AND PROXIMAL SMA. 2. MARKED FOCAL NARROWING OF THE COMMON DUCT WITHIN THE PANCREATIC HEAD LIKELY SECONDARY TO DISEASE INVOLVEMENT. 3. INCREASED DISTENTION OF THE DUODENUM PROXIMAL TO THE MASS WITH ABRUPT NARROWING OF THE DUODENUM SEGMENT AT THE LEVEL OF THE MASS, SUSPICIOUS FOR DUODENAL INVASION BY PANCREATIC CARCINOMA WITH PARTIAL DUODENAL OBSTRUCTION. MAJOR FINDINGS WERE CONVEYED TO andrea larsen IN THE ED ON november 2002. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Where:

- "status" is:

  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: There is bibasilar atelectasis present. There is no evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion. Redemonstrated is high-density material noted in the segment 6 of the liver lesion, postchemoembolization, consistent with Ethiodol. There is a small focus of Ethiodol noted at the anterior dome of the liver (2000/3 / 15). There is a small focus of enhancement in the right lobe of the liver (2000-5-19) without convincing washout, likely consistent with an arteriovenous shunt. There has been interval increase in the ascites when compared to previous examination. Additionally, the fluid around the gallbladder fossa has also increased. There is nodularity of the liver surface consistent with liver cirrhosis. There is thickening of the ascending colon that extends to the cecal tip with minimal associated fat stranding that could be consistent with a focal colitis versus portal colopathy. The pancreas and adrenals are unremarkable. There is spleen is enlarged and is unchanged from previous examination. Again seen are multiple, too small to characterize, hypodensities of both kidneys, likely consistent with cysts, also unchanged. A left interpolar cyst of the left kidney is also unchanged. The small bowel is of normal caliber without evidence of small bowel obstruction. The portal and hepatic veins as well as superior mesenteric vein are patent. The hepatic arteries are unremarkable. There are multiple varices noted in the paraesophageal, perigastric, and perisplenic region. There are no suspicious osseous lesions seen. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL INCREASE IN THE ASCITES AS WELL AS FLUID AROUND THE GALLBLADDER FOSSA. EXTENSIVE COLLATERAL, LIVER CIRRHOSIS AND STIGMATA OF PORTAL HYPERTENSION. 2. EDEMA OF THE ASCENDING COLON AND CECAL TIP, LIKELY FOCAL COLITIS VERSUS PORTAL COLOPATHY. 3. POSTCHEMOEMBOLIZATION OF SEGMENT 6 OF THE LIVER LESION, STABLE. 4. SMALL FOCUS OF ENHANCEMENT IN SEGMENT 6 OF THE LIVER, LIKELY A SHUNT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Evaluation of the abdominal viscera is limited by the lack of intravenous contrast. There is mild bibasilar atelectasis. There is moderate centrilobular emphysema. Aortic valvular calcification is present. Coronary atherosclerotic disease is noted. The heart is enlarged. The noncontrast appearance of the liver is normal. A rounded rim calcified gallstone is present in the gallbladder. No pericholecystic inflammatory changes are seen. No biliary ductal dilatation. The noncontrast appearance of the pancreas, adrenal glands, and spleen is normal. There is an obstructing calculus, measuring 10 mm in greatest length, in the proximal left ureter (series 4, image 173), causing moderate hydronephrosis and proximal hydroureter as well as moderate perinephric fat stranding. There is a partially duplicated right renal collecting system. Negative for right sided hydronephrosis, urolithiasis, or significant perinephric stranding. There are stable postsurgical changes of total cystectomy with ileal conduit in the right lower quadrant. There is a large hiatal hernia. There is marked descending and sigmoid colonic diverticulosis, without evidence for acute diverticulitis. The ileo-ileal anastomosis is dilated, consistent with expected surgical change. There is otherwise no bowel dilatation to suggest obstruction. The appendix appears normal. No free peritoneal air. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are seen in the abdomen and pelvis. No ascites is seen. There are moderate degenerative changes in the lower lumbar spine. No change in mild multilevel compression deformity of multiple visualized thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies. Mild thoracolumbar scoliosis is noted. No worrisome bone lesions are seen. IMPRESSION: 1. OBSTRUCTING 10-MM CALCULUS IN THE PROXIMAL LEFT URETER CAUSING MODERATE LEFT-SIDED HYDRONEPHROSIS. ASSOCIATED LEFT PERINEPHRIC FAT STRANDING COULD BE SECONDARY TO OBSTRUCTION OR COULD REFLECT SUPERIMPOSED INFECTION. 2. MARKED COLONIC DIVERTICULOSIS WITHOUT EVIDENCE FOR ACUTE DIVERTICULITIS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: A 3-mm pulmonary nodule seen in the left lower lobe (series 3 image 6), which appears unchanged compared with the prior study from February 13, 2004. No new pulmonary nodules identified. There is no evidence of pleural or pericardial effusions. Several small low-attenuation lesions are seen in the liver; the larger lesions measure simple fluid attenuation compatible with simple cysts, while some of the smaller lesions are too small to definitively characterize. These lesions are poorly visualized on the prior study without intravenous contrast. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. An area of focal fat is noted adjacent to the fissure of the ligamentum teres. The gallbladder appears decompressed with focal wall thickening in the area of the gallbladder fundus and parts of the body. The spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable. Small low-attenuation renal lesions likely represent cysts. Small bowel loops in the pelvis appear mildly hyper enhancing and edematous, which may relate to prior radiation therapy. A small amount of free fluid is seen in the pelvis. There is no evidence of small bowel obstruction. A normal caliber appendix with small appendicolith is seen in the right lower quadrant. There is no evidence of periappendiceal stranding. The colon appears unremarkable. The uterus and ovaries are surgically absent. The urinary bladder appears decompressed, but is otherwise unremarkable. Extensive atherosclerotic calcification is seen within the abdominal aorta, common iliac arteries, and more distal branches. Incidental note is made of a circumaortic left renal vein. No evidence of pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen or pelvis. Clips are noted along the pelvic sidewall, likely related to oophorectomy and lymphadenectomy. Evaluation of the osseous structures demonstrates diffuse osteopenia, but no suspicious lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. POSTSURGICAL CHANGES IN THE PELVIS WITH NO DEFINITE EVIDENCE OF RESIDUAL OR RECURRENT DISEASE. 2. MILD WALL THICKENING AND MUCOSAL HYPEREMIA IS SEEN WITHIN SMALL BOWEL LOOPS IN THE PELVIS, WHICH MAY RELATE TO PRIOR RADIATION THERAPY. COMPARISON WITH THE PRIOR STUDY IS LIMITED BY THE LACK OF INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST AT THAT TIME. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION. 3. GALLBLADDER WALL THICKENING, WHICH IS MORE PROMINENT AND FOCAL IN THE GALLBLADDER FUNDUS, LIKELY REPRESENTING ADENOMYOMATOSIS. THIS COULD BE CONFIRMED WITH ULTRASOUND IF DESIRED. 4. A PREVIOUSLY DESCRIBED MIXED SOLID AND GROUNDGLASS LESION IN THE RIGHT UPPER LOBE IS NOT INCLUDED ON THE CURRENT STUDY; IF FOLLOW-UP OF THIS AREA IS ALSO DESIRED, INCLUSION OF THE CHEST COULD BE REQUESTED ON THE NEXT FOLLOW-UP STUDY. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is interlobular septal thickening and bronchial wall thickening, as well as diffuse patchy ground-glass opacities at the lung bases. There is a small right pleural effusion. Findings likely represent pulmonary edema. The heart appears enlarged. There are elevated right heart pressures with reflux of contrast into the IVC and hepatic veins. No pericardial effusion is demonstrated. The liver is normal in size and contour. No intrahepatic mass is demonstrated. No intra-or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation is present. The gallbladder demonstrates a diffusely thickened wall. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. There is periportal edema. A small amount of perihepatic ascites is present. The pancreas demonstrates normal lobulation and homogeneous contrast enhancement. There is no pancreatic ductal dilatation. The spleen is normal in size. There is a nonspecific 6 mm hypoattenuating focus within the upper pole of the spleen. There is minimal perisplenic free fluid. The splenic vein, superior mesenteric vein, and inferior mesenteric vein are patent. The adrenal glands appear normal. The kidneys are normal in size. There are large, wedge-shaped areas of hypoattenuation involving multiple segments of the left kidney, forming a nearly confluent zone of hypovascularity in the upper pole. These regions remain hypoattenuating on delayed phases, compatible with renal infarction. More subtle areas of linear hypoattenuation are present within the right kidney and may represent similar, more focal areas of renal infarction. There is no hydronephrosis, renal calculus, or renal mass. There is no obstruction to the excretion of contrast on delayed images. The urinary bladder demonstrates normal wall thickness. There is a small amount of free fluid in the pelvis, with attenuation suggesting a small amount of hemoperitoneum. The uterus appears grossly normal. A right corpus luteum cyst is present. The left ovary appears normal. The abdominal aorta is patent and normal in caliber. The celiac, superior mesenteric, renal, inferior mesenteric, and iliac arteries are patent. The distal esophagus, stomach, small bowel, and colon are normal in caliber and wall thickness. The appendix appears normal. The colon is normal in caliber. Minimal fluid is present in the right lower quadrant, in the paracolic gutter. No free air is present. There is no adenopathy in the abdomen, pelvis, or inguinal regions, by CT size criteria. However, mesenteric and paraaortic nodes, measuring up to 8 mm, are present, most prominent within the right lower quadrant of the mesentery. The osseous structures appear normal. There is a tiny, fat-containing umbilical hernia. A tiny right inguinal hernia is also present. IMPRESSION: 1. LARGE LEFT RENAL INFARCTS, WITH PROBABLE TINY RIGHT RENAL AND SPLENIC INFARCTS AS WELL. 2. CARDIOMEGALY WITH PULMONARY EDEMA AND RIGHT PLEURAL EFFUSION INDICATE CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Incidentally noted gynecomastia. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The distal descending thoracic aorta is normal in course and caliber. The distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction are grossly normal. No pleural effusions. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates minimal dependent, reticular and ground glass opacities compatible with atelectasis. The liver is normal in size, contour and attenuation. No focal hepatic mass. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. The gallbladder appears normal without evidence of radiopaque gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid. The adrenal glands appear normal. The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. The pancreas appears normal without evidence of mass or ductal dilatation. The kidneys are symmetric in size, contour, contrast enhancement and excretion. No focal renal mass, hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis. Multiple right upper pole renal cysts are seen. The bladder is collapsed limiting evaluation. Heterogeneously enhancing prostate with soft tissue protruding into the bladder most likely median lobe hypertrophy related to benign prostatic hyperplasia. The seminal vesicles appear normal. A gastrostomy tube is present. A duodenal diverticulum arises from the second portion of the duodenum, without evidence of diverticulitis. The small bowel is normal without evidence of bowel wall thickening, hyperemia, pneumatosis or obstruction. Apparent wall thickening in the rectum is likely secondary to lack of distention given lack of surrounding inflammation. The dilated bowel seen on prior KUB reflects a redundant loop of sigmoid colon with the largest diameter measuring up to 14cm located in the right lower quadrant. The appendix is within normal limits. No free fluid within the abdomen or pelvis. The abdominal aorta is normal in course and caliber with atherosclerosis. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes within the abdomen or pelvis. Anterior wedge deformity at L1, likely chronic. Likely chronic bilateral inferior pubic rami fractures are seen. Osteopenia is seen. IMPRESSION: 1. NO BOWEL OBSTRUCTION. QUERIED LOOP OF DILATED BOWEL CORRESPONDS TO REDUNDANT LOOP OF SIGMOID COLON. 2. HETEROGENEOUS PROSTATE WITH LIKELY MEDIAN LOBE HYPERTROPHY RELATED TO BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERPLASIA. CORRELATE WITH PSA AND CLINICAL EXAM IF CLINICALLY INDICATED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Small pleural effusions bilaterally with associated atelectasis, new from prior. Stable size of gas and fluid collection in segment 6 measuring up to 3.0 x 3.2 cm, now status post drain which appears to terminate in the collection. Small amount of perihepatic fluid containing gas at drain entry site measuring 2.6 x 0.5 cm. Curvilinear tubular focus of fluid attenuation up to 12 mm in diameter and approximately 7 cm in length which appears to communicate with abscess as it contains gas and extends to inferior liver tip. Redemonstration of marked heterogeneity of liver particularly with patchy areas of hypoattenuation near hilum particularly in segment 4b. Gallbladder surgically absent. No intrahepatic or extra hepatic biliary ductal dilatation. Pneumobilia. Mild to moderate splenomegaly. Bilateral adrenal glands without focal lesion. Bilateral kidneys without focal lesion, stones, or hydronephrosis. Evidence of prior Whipple procedure. Air in pancreatic duct. Bowel normal in caliber. Appendix seen within right paracolic gutter. Mild increase in small amount of ascites particularly and pelvis without definite peritoneal or enhancement. Small amount of stranding in mesenteric root. Bladder unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy. No suspicious osseous lesions. Presumed bone islands in the right hemipelvis and right femoral head. IMPRESSION: 1. REDEMONSTRATION OF GAS AND FLUID COLLECTION IN SEGMENT 6 MEASURING UP TO 3.0 X 3.2 CM, NOW STATUS POST PLACEMENT OF DRAIN WHICH APPEARS TO TERMINATE IN THE COLLECTION. HOWEVER, INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF A CURVILINEAR TUBULAR FOCUS OF FLUID ATTENUATION MEASURING APPROXIMATELY 7 CM IN LENGTH AND UP TO 12 MM IN DIAMETER AS IT COMMUNICATES WITH THE ABSCESS, EXTENDS TO INFERIOR LIVER TIP, AND CONTAINS GAS, POSSIBLY REPRESENTING A NEW COMPONENT OF THE ABSCESS. 2. SMALL AMOUNT OF PERIHEPATIC FLUID CONTAINING GAS AT DRAIN ENTRY SITE MEASURING 2.6 X 0.5 CM, LIKELY POSTPROCEDURAL. 3. STRANDING AT MESENTERIC ROOT, LIKELY POSTSURGICAL, STATUS POST WHIPPLE PROCEDURE. 4. MILD INCREASE IN ASCITES PARTICULARLY IN THE PELVIS, HOWEVER WITHOUT DEFINITE PERITONEAL OR RIM ENHANCEMENT TO SUGGEST PERITONITIS OR INFECTION AT THIS TIME. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is atherosclerotic disease of the descending and abdominal aorta. Mild to moderate narrowing of the origin of the celiac axis is identified as well as at the origin of the SMA. The splenic artery is tortuous. The IMA is patent. Extensive varices are seen in the esophageal, gastric, splenic and splenorenal regions. On the delayed phase images there is no contrast enhancement of the splenic vein compatible the patient's known history of splenic vein thrombosis. There is prominence of the portal vein likely compatible with portal hypertension. The lung bases are clear and there are no pleural effusions. Visualized portions of the heart are normal in appearance and size. No pericardial effusion. Evaluation of the abdominal solid organs demonstrate calcifications within the pancreas consistent with chronic pancreatitis. No cystic lesion is identified to suggest current pseudocyst. There is mild prominence of the pancreatic duct. The liver is normal in appearance, without evidence of periportal edema or intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The spleen is not significantly enlarged. The kidneys and adrenal glands are unremarkable.. No nephrolithiasis or hydronephrosis. Uncomplicated diverticulosis is identified without evidence to suggest diverticulitis. The bladder is unremarkable. Calcification of the prostate is identified. No ascites or pneumoperitoneum. No abdominal, pelvic, or inguinal lymphadenopathy identified. Numerous sclerotic osseous lesions are identified scattered throughout the ribs, and spine, pelvis and femoral heads compatible with the patient's known metastasis from prostate cancer.     "related": ["str (field:status)"]


Where:

- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.

Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: VASCULATURE: A 5-cm dissection of the SMA with partial thrombosis of the false lumen is identified (image 208, series 4). Branches of the SMA arise from both the false and true lumen. The celiac artery, renal arteries, and inferior mesenteric artery are patent. Mild atherosclerosis is identified in the aorta. The aorta is normal caliber. A prominent arterial vessel is noted in the rectum, without evidence of extravasation (image 576, series 3). CHEST: Evaluation of the lung bases demonstrates minimal bibasilar atelectasis. No evidence of a pleural effusion. Limited evaluation of the heart demonstrates normal heart size. No pericardial effusions. ABDOMEN: Evaluation of the abdominal structures limited due to arterial contrast phase image acquisition. A small to moderate hiatal hernia is present. A 2.3 x 4.3 cm cyst is identified in the left lobe of the liver. A 1 cm hemangioma is noted in the lateral right lobe of the liver (image 98, series 3). No evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is unremarkable, without evidence of wall thickening. The spleen, pancreas, and right adrenal are unremarkable. A 9 mm left adrenal nodule is noted (image 180, series 4). A 4.1 cm cyst is identified in the upper pole of the right kidney. A 2.8 cm intermediate density lesion is identified medial to the upper pole the left kidney, which is not definitely associated with the kidney measuring 37 Hounsfield units (image 222, series 4). No evidence of hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis. There is no significant bowel wall thickening, although evaluation limited due to arterial phase acquisition. Scattered colonic diverticuli noted, without evidence of diverticulitis. No evidence of a bleeding diverticulum. The urinary bladder is partially decompressed, limiting evaluation. The prostate is enlarged measuring up to 5.7 cm. A 6-mm left pelvic sidewall node is noted (image 611, series 3). No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes identified in the abdomen and pelvis. No evidence of free fluid or free intraperitoneal air. No suspicious osseous lesions identified. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: 52013 Diameters in mm Major Minor SMA maximum diameter 15.40 12.30 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. LONG SEGMENT DISSECTION OF THE SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY AS ABOVE WITH PARTIAL THROMBOSIS OF THE FALSE LUMEN. 2. PROMINENT ARTERIAL VASCULATURE IN THE RECTUM, WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF ACTIVE EXTRAVASATION. NO SIGNIFICANT BOWEL WALL THICKENING, ALTHOUGH EVALUATION LIMITED DUE TO ARTERIAL PHASE IMAGING. NO EVIDENCE OF BLEEDING DIVERTICULUM OR AVM. 3. INCOMPLETELY EVALUATED SOFT TISSUE DENSITY MEDIAL TO THE UPPER POLE OF THE LEFT KIDNEY WHICH IS INDETERMINATE. RECOMMEND CORRELATION WITH PRIOR OUTSIDE STUDIES IF AVAILABLE. 4. SMALL 9 MM LEFT ADRENAL NODULE NOTED, WHICH IS INCOMPLETELY EVALUATED ON THE CURRENT STUDY. 5. SMALL TO MODERATE HIATAL HERNIA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION FINDINGS WAS DISCUSSED WITH Kayla Shubrick BY THE ON-CALL RESIDENT Dr Madden Owen ON february 19th, 2004 AT 11:45 PM. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: LUNG BASES: The lung bases are clear and there are no pleural effusions. A tiny right lower lobe calcified granuloma is demonstrated. Visualized portions of the heart are normal in appearance and size. The blood pool is hypoattenuated in comparison to the myocardium, suggesting anemia. No pericardial effusion. ABDOMEN / PELVIS: Evaluation of the abdominal solid organs is suboptimal due to the arterial phase of imaging. The liver is markedly shrunken and nodular in contour with evidence of portal hypertension, including massively enlarged superior mesenteric venous-renal shunt and splenomegaly. The main, right, and left portal veins are patent but are very diminutive. The superior mesenteric, hepatic, and splenic veins are patent without evidence of thrombus. Small esophageal varices are noted. There has been marked decrease in ascites, now small in volume. An umbilical hernia containing fluid and fat is again noted. Layering gallstones are noted within the gallbladder and in the cystic duct. There is no evidence of acute cholecystitis. The pancreas and adrenal glands are normal in appearance. There is a dense nephrogram on the unenhanced phase, suggesting renal disease. No nephrolithiasis or hydronephrosis. The small and large bowel are normal in course and caliber. The bladder is partially decompressed by a Foley catheter. Foci of air within the bladder is likely due to recent instrumentation. No abdominal, pelvic, or inguinal lymphadenopathy identified. The visualized aorta, celiac artery, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery are normal in course and caliber. The common hepatic artery arises directly from the aorta. There are single bilateral renal arteries, both normal in course and caliber. No suspicious bony lesions identified. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: 346-913-759-696 Diameters in mm Major Minor Splenic artery aneurysm1-superior aneurysm 11.20 9.60 Splenic artery aneurysm2-inferior aneurysm 6.70 6.00 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends Addendum Begins Following additional 3-D reconstructions, two small splenic artery aneurysms are noted at the splenic hilum, measuring approximately 10mm and 6 mm (series 3, images 149, 164, and series 252, image 4). Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. CIRRHOSIS WITH EVIDENCE OF PORTAL HYPERTENSION, INCLUDING MASSIVELY ENLARGED SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VENOUS-RENAL SHUNT TO THE RIGHT RENAL VEIN. PATENT BUT DIMINUTIVE MAIN, RIGHT, AND LEFT PORTAL VEINS. SUPERIOR MESENTERIC, HEPATIC, AND SPLENIC VEINS ARE ALSO PATENT. THE COMMON HEPATIC ARTERY ARISES DIRECTLY FROM THE ABDOMINAL AORTA. 2. MARKED DECREASED ASCITES, NOW SMALL IN VOLUME. 3. CHOLELITHIASIS WITH STONES ALSO NOTED IN THE CYSTIC DUCT. 4. DENSE NEPHROGRAM ON THE UNENHANCED PHASE, COMPATIBLE WITH RENAL DISEASE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Lower thorax: Mild bibasilar subsegmental atelectasis. No other significant pulmonary, pleural, or mediastinal abnormality is seen in the visualized portions of the thorax. Liver: Stable tiny low density lesion, too small to characterize. Otherwise, normal. Gallbladder: Normal. Spleen: Normal. Pancreas: Normal. Adrenal glands: Normal. Kidneys: Stable punctate low density lesions within the kidneys, which are too small to accurately characterize but likely represent cysts. The kidneys are otherwise normal in size and appearance. Normal excretion on delayed images. Bowel: Colonic diverticula are seen predominantly within the sigmoid colon. There is a focal segment of sigmoid colon demonstrating circumferential wall thickening and surrounding fat stranding (9/20/00/25, 601/60) measuring up to 8 cm in length. A multi-loculated gas and fluid collection with rim enhancement is identified immediately adjacent to the affected sigmoid colon measuring up to 3.2 x 4.2 cm in size (4/299) consistent with an abscess. Small foci of free gas is seen adjacent to the affected colon. Given the size and location of this collection, this lesion would not be amenable to percutaneous drainage at this time. The stomach is mildly distended with gas and ingested material. The small and large bowel is of normal caliber without evidence of obstruction. The appendix is normal in appearance. Peritoneal space: Multiple tiny foci of extraluminal gas are identified consistent with small pneumoperitoneum. Small free fluid is seen in Morrison's pouch and right paracolic gutter. Mild peritoneal enhancement suggests peritonitis. Vasculature: The aorta is normal in course and caliber. The major abdominal vasculature is patent. Lymph nodes: No enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes. Pelvic organs: The urinary bladder is normal. The prostate and seminal vesicles are normal. Musculoskeletal: No aggressive osseous lesion is identified in the abdomen or pelvis. IMPRESSION: 1. RECURRENT SIGMOID DIVERTICULITIS NOW WITH EVIDENCE OF PERFORATION WITH SMALL PNEUMOPERITONEUM AND FREE FLUID. A SMALL MULTILOCULATED ABSCESS IS SEEN ADJACENT TO THE AFFECTED COLON. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Overall, no interval change in metastatic ovarian carcinoma; no new metastases identified. 1. No change in large, loculated, nondependent right pleural effusion with associated pleural thickening. Some apparent intrinsic high density within bilateral pleural surfaces (seen more prominently on the left side) may reflect sequela of prior talc pleurodesis. A mild nondependent, loculated left pleural effusion (tracking along the major fissure) is likewise unchanged. 2. No change in malignant ascites, with multiple peritoneal and serosal tumor implants and scalloped contour of liver (consistent with tumor implants upon the liver surface) redemonstrated. The peritoneal tumor implants, which are solid and enhancing, are seen most prominently within the pelvis, measuring up to 2.1 x 1.7 cm within the left pelvis (image 66, series 3), and up to 2.8 x 2.0 cm within the right pelvis (image 67, series 3)--both unchanged compared with 11/07/01. No new peritoneal tumor implants are seen. 3. No change in multiple ill-defined, low density liver metastases measuring up to 2.2 cm within liver segment 5 (image 16, series 3); no new liver metastases. A small component of this inferolaterally measures 1.5 x 2.6 cm. This could represent a small periappendiceal abscess, although simple reactive fluid could have a similar appearance. There is a small amount of free fluid in the pelvis, as well as within Morison's pouch. There is no free air. There are multiple small retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes, none of which meets criteria for pathologic enlargement. There is a mild amount of periaortic fat stranding of uncertain etiology. Heart size is normal. There is no pericardial or pleural effusion. There is a small amount of subsegmental atelectasis in the right lower lobe. Lungs are otherwise unremarkable. Regional osseous structures demonstrate bilateral pars interarticularis defects at L5-S1 without anterolisthesis. IMPRESSION: 1. FINDINGS ARE CONSISTENT WITH ACUTE APPENDICITIS. 2. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates a calcified nodule in the right lower lobe, which is most likely the sequela of prior granulomatous disease. The liver is normal in size, contour, and attenuation. Region of hypoattenuation is seen along the medial segment of the left lobe of the liver that is likely due to focal fatty infiltration. Several well circumscribed round hypodense lesions measuring up to 8 mm are seen in the liver series 4, image 119, image 76, and image 56; these lesionsare too small to characterize definitively but likely represent cysts. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. The gallbladder appears normal without evidence of radiopaque gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid. The adrenal glands appear normal. The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. The pancreas appears normal without evidence of mass or ductal dilatation. The kidneys are symmetric in size, contour, contrast enhancement and excretion. No focal renal mass, hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis. There is a duplicated right renal artery. The bladder, prostate and seminal vesicles appear normal. The stomach appears normal. The small bowel is normal in caliber and wall thickness. There is inspissated fecal material within the small bowel that is likely secondary to delayed transit. The colon is normal in course and caliber. There are two regions of focal colonic wall thickening within the sigmoid colon and splenic flexure that are likely secondary to peristalsis as there is neither fatty infiltration nor lymphadenopathy in the region. The appendix is within normal limits. No free fluid within the abdomen or pelvis. The abdominal aorta is normal in course and caliber. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes within the abdomen or pelvis. There are acute mildly displaced fractures of the right transverse processes of L1-L5 series 4, image 251, image 229, image 202, image 178, and image 151. There is a small minimally displaced fracture of the right sacrum adjacent to the anteriorr aspect of the sacroiliac joint series 4, image 307. There is a nondisplaced fracture of the right superior pelvic ramus series 4, image 366. Addendum Begins Impression should state: IMPRESSION: 1. input: FINDINGS: The lung bases are essentially clear. Minimal atelectasis is noted. Respiratory motion somewhat limits evaluation. Heart size is normal. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. There is diffuse low background attenuation of the liver compatible with fatty infiltration. A 1.3-cm hypodense lesion is seen within segment 4 near the liver dome. Although it is somewhat greater attenuation than expected for water, it is too small to characterize and may represent a tiny cyst. No additional liver lesion is identified. A 1.5-cm gallstone layers dependently within the gallbladder body / neck. No complication is identified. There is no biliary ductal dilatation. The spleen is unremarkable. Pancreas is mildly atrophic but has an otherwise unremarkable appearance. Adrenal glands are normal. There are subcentimeter hypodensities within the right kidney which are too small to characterize. Left renal sinus cysts are incidentally noted. The kidneys are otherwise unremarkable without hydronephrosis, solid mass lesion, or perinephric collection. Postsurgical changes related to ventral hernia repair are noted. Two sites of herniated small bowel are seen extending anteriorly to the ventral abdominal wall. One loop extends through a defect between the left rectus abdominis and the left aspect of the mesh repair (3/171). There is associated wall thickening of this loop as it enters the hernia with mesenteric stranding (4/155) concerning for strangulation. There is also upstream dilatation of the immediately proximal small bowel up to 3 cm compatible with obstruction. Trace ascites layers within the pelvis also concerning for early strangulation. No free air is identified to suggest perforation. The second hernia is more midline in location and contains small bowel loops distal to the possible strangulated hernia but is widemouthed without evidence of complication. It extends through portions of the mesh repair (3/169) and continues to extend the anteriorly to the intra-abdominal wall but contained within remainder of the mesh. Numerous colonic diverticula are identified without complication. Small bowel loops distal to the hernia are collapsed. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. The patient is status post prostatectomy. input: FINDINGS: Scout localizer images demonstrate a patent upper cervical spine. Stable postsurgical changes are seen involving the left temporal bone and underlying left temporal lobe with stable appearance of the resection cavity with faint linear areas of surrounding enhancement. There is no focal nodular enhancement to suggest residual or recurrent disease. Old blood products along the resection cavity, as well as siderosis within the posterior fossa are stable. There is associated stable marked ex vacuo dilatation of the left lateral ventricle, with minimal surrounding parenchymal signal intensity changes within the left temporal and parietal lobes. Scattered nonspecific foci of white matter signal intensity changes within the right periatrial white matter are also stable. There is no intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or acute infarct. The paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are well formed and aerated. The demonstrated orbits are within normal limits. Perfusion imaging is without significant change in the perfusion maps. IMPRESSION: STABLE POSTSURGICAL AND POSTTREATMENT RELATED CHANGES, TO INCLUDE POSTERIOR FOSSA SIDEROSIS, WITHOUT DEFINITE FINDINGS OF RESIDUAL OR RECURRENT DISEASE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Scattered areas of thickening and T2 / FLAIR hyperintensity predominantly involving the cortices of the bilateral frontal, parietal, occipital, and temporal lobes across multiple arterial territories. Some of these areas are associated with restricted diffusion, most notably within the medial left occipital lobe and bilateral posterior parietal lobes. The left superior cerebellum also demonstrates restricted diffusion. The bilateral hippocampi are spared. Additional signal abnormalities with associated restricted diffusion are identified in the bilateral, right much greater than left, borderzone white matter territories. No intracranial hemorrhage is identified. The ventricles are symmetric in size and configuration. Pan sinus mucosal thickening is seen. Minimal right mastoid effusion is present. MRA images of the intracranial circulation demonstrate no significant steno-occlusive disease nor cerebral aneurysm. No evidence of venous sinus thrombosis is present on MRV images. In addition, ASL perfusion images demonstrates asymmetric high cerebral blood flow throughout the right cerebral hemisphere and medial left occipital lobe, possibly related to luxury reperfusion. IMPRESSION: 1. Heterogeneous areas of cortical thickening, T2 hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion throughout the bilateral cerebral hemispheres. Restricted diffusion also present in the left superior cerebellum and bilateral borderzone territories, right greater than left. Constellation of these findings may be related to multiple arterial infarcts related to hypoperfusion although these findings are not classic for this diagnosis. Alternatively, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) may be considered although these findings are unusual given extent of restricted diffusion and cortical involvement. Less likely etiologies include epiphenomenon related to seizure activity, encephalitis, and venous infarcts. 2. Consider follow-up MRI with contrast administration (if possible) in a few days to assess for evolution of findings. This is provided that the patient does not clinically deteriorate in the interim. 3. Findings were discussed with Huerta Dr on 1/31/2004 at 7:40 PM SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Post-surgical changes are present compatible with prior transphenoidal surgery. There are no new areas of abnormal signal nor enhancement to suggest new disease. The parasellar regions are clear with no abnormality of the cavernous sinus, paranasal sinuses, nor optic chiasm. There is no abnormality of the clivus, nasal cavity, nor orbits. The pituitary gland demonstrates normal signal intensity, size, and homogeneous enhancement. The pituitary stalk is midline. There is no discrete pituitary mass consistent with a microadenoma or macroadenoma. Redemonstration lace like enhancement along the left pons, consistent with a capillary telangiectasia. Linear enhancement is seen extending from the anterior to posterior pons, likely representing a developmental venous anomaly. There is no abnormal signal nor enhancement of the brain parenchyma. No restricted diffusion is present to suggest recent ischemic injury. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage. The ventricles are appropriate in size and symmetric in configuration. The mastoid air cells are clear. Intracranial vessels are patent. IMPRESSION: NO SIGNIFICANT CHANGE IN THE APPEARANCE OF THE PITUITARY GLAND AND POSTSURGICAL CHANGES COMPARED TO 2001/11. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Study is slightly motion degraded. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, infarct, mass, mass effect, or edema. A chronic left internal capsule infarct is noted. Post-surgical changes are present following a left frontoparietal craniotomy, with a left EC-IC bypass. The ventricles and sulci are stable in size and configuration. Punctate old micro hemorrhage within the left temporal lobe is stable. On MRA, the left internal carotid artery is diminutive in caliber diffusely, with high grade stenosis or occlusion of the supraclinoid segment, along with absent flow related enhancement within the left A1 and M1 segments of the anterior and middle cerebral arteries respectively, associated with moyamoya collaterals. These findings appear similar to the prior MRI, though on the diagnostic cerebral angiogram was noted to be progressed in severity. The left EC-IC bypass is widely patent. No steno-occlusive disease of the right anterior or posterior circulation is noted. On perfusion imaging, there is mild T-max prolongation within the left watershed and MCA zones, similar to prior study. Estimated cerebral blood flow to the whole brain is approximately 45 mL / 100 g / min. Following diamox administration, there is whole brain augmentation to approximately 57 mL / 100g / min. This represents an approximately 27% increase. Estimated cerebral blood flow to the left MCA territory is approximately 36 mL / 100g / min, which augments to approximately 50 mL / 100g / min. IMPRESSION: 1. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. NO ACUTE INFARCT. 2. CHRONIC LEFT INTERNAL CAPSULE INFARCT. 3. POST-SURGICAL CHANGES OF A LEFT EC-IC BYPASS, WHICH IS PATENT. 4. NATIVE UNILATERAL LEFT MOYA MOYA TYPE VASCULOPATHY, WITH SEVERE STENOSIS OR OCCLUSION OF THE LEFT SUPRACLINOID ICA, WITH ABSENT FLOW IN THE A1 AND M1 SEGMENTS OF THE ANTERIOR AND MIDDLE CEREBRAL ARTERIES, RESPECTIVELY, ASSOCIATED WITH MOYA MOYA TYPE COLLATERALS. 5. MILD PERFUSION ABNORMALITY TO THE LEFT MCA AND WATERSHED ZONES. 6. FOLLOWING DIAMOX ADMINISTRATION, THERE IS EVIDENCE OF APPROPRIATE AUGMENTATION OF THE WHOLE BRAIN AND WITHIN THE LEFT MCA TERRITORY. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Post operative changes are decreased after occipital craniotomy for pineal lesion resection. There has been interval resolution of the suboccipital extracranial fluid collection. Small infratentorial fluid collection is also smaller in size, but there is minimally increased enhancement along the tentorium (see series 12/10/97); no restricted diffusion is present in this region to suggest infection. There is minimal T2 hyperintensity in the cerebellum along the operative trajectory; otherwise, the brain parenchyma is normal in signal without hemorrhage, mass, nor mass-effect. There is no evidence of residual or recurrent pineal lesion. There are no regions of abnormal enhancement within the brain parenchyma. The ventricles and extra-axial fluid spaces are normal in size and symmetric in configuration. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. There are normal vascular flow voids at the base of the brain. IMPRESSION: 1. EVOLVING POSTOPERATIVE CHANGES STATUS POST OCCIPITAL CRANIOTOMY AND PINEAL LESION RESECTION. INFRATENTORIAL FLUID COLLECTION IS SMALLER AND DEMONSTRATES MINIMAL SURROUNDING ENHANCEMENT. SUBOCCIPITAL FLUID COLLECTION HAS RESOLVED. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF RESIDUAL NOR RECURRENT PINEAL MASS. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There has been interval resection of the T2-hyperintense lesion within the medial aspect of the right cerebellum, consistent with the cavernoma resection. There are blood products within the operative bed, and expected thin rim of postoperative restricted diffusion. However, there is a 3.4 x 1.9-cm area of restricted diffusion posterior to the resection cavity in the right cerebellar hemisphere concerning for infarct. A smaller, but similar area of restricted diffusion is just anterior and inferior to the resection cavity, measuring 11 x 11 mm in diameter. Supratentorially, there are several small areas of increased T2 signal in a periventricular distribution. The ventricles and sulci are within normal size and configuration. No intracranial mass is identified. Normal intracranial intravascular flow-voids are seen. The orbits, periorbital and pericavernous spaces are normal. IMPRESSION: 1. TWO AREAS WITHIN THE RIGHT CEREBELLAR HEMISPHERE ADJACENT TO THE RESECTION CAVITY CONCERNING FOR ACUTE INFARCT, THE LARGEST MEASURING 3.4 X 1.9 CM. 2. PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER CHANGES, IN THIS AGE RANGE MOST LIKELY REPRESENTING SMALL VESSEL ISCHEMIC DISEASE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: MRI BRAIN: Left posterior temporal white matter lesion best seen on series 6, image 14 and minimal amount of nonspecific periventricular patchy white matter changes. No evidence of associated abnormal brain parenchymal enhancement. No evidence of acute infarct or acute intracranial hemorrhage. No mass effect or midline shift. No evidence of extracerebral collections. Visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. MRI OF THE CERVICAL SPINE: Patchy focal area of enhancement within the anterior medulla best seen on series 2, image 7 and series 4, image 7. Numerous patchy areas of abnormal signal seen within the cervical spinal cord which predominantly appear to have a peripheral distribution within the spinal cord and are best appreciated on the sagittal T2 and sagittal STIR images. More focal spinal cord lesion with abnormal signal at the T2 level. No evidence of associated definite enhancement. Alignment of the cervical spine is within normal limits. Vertebral heights and intravertebral discs are well preserved. No evidence of focal disc herniation, central canal stenosis or neural foraminal stenosis. IMPRESSION: 1. MINIMAL AMOUNT OF NONSPECIFIC PATCHY PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER CHANGE AND LEFT POSTERIOR TEMPORAL WHITE MATTER LESION WHICH IS NONSPECIFIC BUT MAY BE RELATED TO THE PATIENT'S CLINICALLY REPORTED MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS. NO EVIDENCE OF ASSOCIATED ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT. 2. EXTENSIVE PATCHY SIGNAL ABNORMALITY SEEN THROUGHOUT THE CERVICAL SPINAL CORD, THE UPPER THORACIC CORD, AND WITHIN THE ANTERIOR ASPECT OF THE MEDULLA WHICH PRESUMABLY IS RELATED TO THE PATIENT'S CLINICALLY REPORTED MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS. NO EVIDENCE OF ASSOCIATED ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MRI of the brain demonstrates no significant interval change in findings of several intra-and extracranial masses, corresponding to multifocal recurrence of hemangiopericytoma. The large, lobulated right infratemporal fossa mass is again noted, extending into the right middle cranial fossa as well as the soft tissues of the nasopharynx. There is compression of the right middle ear canal with associated right mastoid effusion. Additionally, the mass is seen extending into the sphenoid sinus. The other large, dominant lesions in the right temporal pole and right posterior temporal lobe are again seen. There is no significant interval change in the amount of right parietotemporal vasogenic edema. There is a persistent 5-mm leftward midline shift. There is no evidence of infarct. IMPRESSION: 1. REDEMONSTRATION OF MULTIFOCAL RECURRENT INTRA-AND EXTRACRANIAL TUMORS. NO SIGNIFICANT INTERVAL CHANGE IN ASSOCIATED VASOGENIC EDEMA OR MIDLINE SHIFT. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: When reviewed in conjunction with recent MRI IAC of DECEMBER 3RD, 2001, there is no abnormal enhancement along the internal auditory canals. Within the remainder of the brain, there is an approximately 7 mm x 7 mm homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass overlying the right posterior frontal lobe, which is most likely reflective of a meningioma. There is no significant mass effect. On the prior exam of 2001 3 DECEMBER, no corresponding edema is seen. There are no additional areas of abnormal enhancement. The ventricles and sulci are stable in size and configuration. There is no significant mucosal thickening or fluid within the paranasal sinuses. On MRA, the intracranial internal carotid, anterior cerebral, and middle cerebral arteries are patent without significant stenoocclusive disease or aneurysm formation. The intracranial vertebral arteries, basilar artery, and posterior cerebral arteries are also patent without significant stenoocclusive disease or aneurysm formation. IMPRESSION: 1. APPROXIMATELY 7 MM HOMOGENEOUSLY ENHANCING EXTRA-AXIAL MASS OVERLYING THE POSTERIOR RIGHT FRONTAL LOBE, MOST LIKELY REFLECTIVE OF A MENINGIOMA. NO SIGNIFICANT ASSOCIATED MASS EFFECT. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF AN INTRACRANIAL ANEURYSM. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage. No areas of restricted diffusion are present to suggest recent ischemic injury. A few scattered punctate foci of T2 / FLAIR signal hyperintensity are identified within the subcortical and deep white matter, which are nonspecific, but most likely reflect sequela of chronic small vessel ischemic disease. On post-gadolinium images, there are no abnormal areas of enhancement. Apparent foci of enhancement within the right frontal (series 5, image 16) and left frontal (series 5, images 14-15) lobes most likely reflect sequela of pulsation artifact from scalp vessels given lack of associated surrounding vasogenic edema on T2 / FLAIR sequences. The ventricles and extra-axial fluid spaces are appropriate in size and symmetric in configuration. Visualized paranasal sinuses are clear. Mastoid air cells are clear. Bony orbits and their contents are symmetric and intact. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain appear patent. Visualized soft tissues of the head and face are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. NO MR EVIDENCE OF BRAIN METASTASES, AS CLINICALLY QUERIED. 2. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: vascular flow voids at base of brain (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: MRI of the brain: There is a 3 cm focus of hemorrhage noted in the left posterior central gyrus region with adjacent area of edema (series 5, image 14). No other areas of hemorrhage are noted. No abnormally enhancing lesions are noted. No evidence of developmental venous anomaly. Visualized venous sinuses appear patent. Mild mass effect and edema adjacent to the hemorrhagic focus. No significant midline shift and the ventricles and extra-axial spaces are otherwise within normal. MRA of vessels comprised of the circle of Willis demonstrates no significant stenosis or occlusion. No evidence of aneurysm or any other vascular lesion. MRI of the cervical spine: Cervical spine alignment is within normal limits. Marrow signal is normal. Some multilevel degenerative changes of the discs are noted with tiny disc bulges. The spinal cord demonstrates normal contour and signal. At the C2-3 level, no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. At the C3-4 level, tiny disc bulge is noted. However, no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. At the C4-5 level, tiny disc bulge is noted. No significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. At the C5-6 level, tiny broad based disc bulge is noted. However, no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. At the C6-7 level, tiny disc bulge is noted. However, no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. At the C7-T1 level, no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing. Incidental note of 10 mm T1 and T2 hyperintense lesion in the right thyroid lobe (series 5, image 20). This most likely represents colloid cyst. No significant cervical lymphadenopathy. The airway is patent. IMPRESSION: 1. MRI OF THE BRAIN DEMONSTRATING 3 CM AREA OF HEMORRHAGE IN THE LEFT POST CENTRAL GYRUS WITH ADJACENT AREA OF EDEMA. RECOMMEND COMPARISON TO ANY PRIOR IMAGES IF AVAILABLE. THERE IS A WIDE DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS LIST FOR SUCH A LESION IN A YOUNG WOMAN, INCLUDING CAVERNOUS MALFORMATION, MICRO-AVM, CORTICAL VENOUS THROMBOSIS, HYPERTENSION, AND DRUG-RELATED. 2. NO DEFINITE ABNORMALLY ENHANCING LESIONS OR ANY OTHER VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS. 3. CERVICAL SPINE DEMONSTRATING TINY DISC BULGES AT MULTIPLE LEVELS AS DESCRIBED ABOVE. HOWEVER, NO SIGNIFICANT SPINAL CANAL STENOSIS OR NEURAL FORAMINAL NARROWING. 4. INCIDENTAL 10 MM LIKELY RIGHT THYROID COLLOID CYST. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: There are interval surgical changes with right temporal scalp edema with fluid and air noted in the right posterior temporal resection bed. There is pachymeningeal enhancement noted along the right temporal cavity, which is likely reactive. There is a small amount of intraventricular hemorrhage which is likely postsurgical. There is no abnormal residual enhancement within this resection bed. Again, there are two areas of nodular enhancement seen inferior to the resection bed, along the lateral aspect of the right middle cranial fossa which are not significantly changed. Again, there is surrounding vasogenic edema which appears unchanged. The ventricles appear stable in size and shape. Again, there are areas of patchy small scattered foci of T2 signal intensity. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage and no midline shift. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL SURGICAL CHANGES WITH FLUID AND AIR SEEN WITHIN THE RIGHT TEMPORAL RESECTION BED WITH ADJACENT PACHYMENINGEAL ENHANCEMENT WHICH IS LIKELY REACTIVE. THERE IS NO ABNORMAL RESIDUAL ENHANCEMENT WITHIN THE RESECTION BED. 2. AGAIN, THERE ARE TWO FOCI OF NODULAR ENHANCEMENT SEEN INFERIOR TO THE RESECTION CAVITY ALONG THE LATERAL ASPECT OF THE RIGHT MIDDLE CRANIAL FOSSA, WHICH ARE PROBABLY NOT CHANGED IN COMPARISON WITH THE PRIOR MRI. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: lateral aspect right middle cranial fossa (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: Accounting for differences in technique, there is a stable-to-slightly-smaller CSF isointense ovoid mass or mass-like lesion in the right cerebellopontine angle extending into the prepontine cistern. There is effacement of the root entry zone and cisternal segment of the right trigeminal nerve. There may be mild contact with the cisternal segment of the right 7th / 8th nerve complex. There is minimal contouring of the pons and cerebellopontine angle. There is no definite associated diffusion restriction. There may be mild rim-like enhancement but no definite intralesional enhancement. The exam is otherwise normal without acute hemorrhage, infarction, mass or hydrocephalus. IMPRESSION: 1. CSF ISOINTENSE MASS OR MASS-LIKE LESION IN THE RIGHT PREPONTINE CISTERN AND CEREBELLOPONTINE ANGLE, WHICH IS LIKELY STABLE-TO-SLIGHTLY DECREASED IN SIZE. THERE IS DEFINITE DIFFUSION RESTRICTION TO CLEARLY IMPLICATE EPIDERMOID, AND THE IMAGING FEATURES ARE NOT SPECIFIC. A LOCULATED CSF COLLECTION AS MIGHT BE SEEN IN THE SETTING OF ARACHNOIDITIS COULD HAVE THIS APPEARANCE. 2. STABLE MASS EFFECT UPON THE ROOT ENTRY ZONE AND CISTERNAL SEGMENT OF THE RIGHT TRIGEMINAL NERVE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Prominent ventricles and sulci are consistent with volume loss. Scattered foci of non-enhancing T2 hyperintensity, some of which are globular, are present predominantly within the bilateral cerebral hemispheres sparing the U-fibers and are non-specific; these foci are not distributed in the typical pattern associated with primary demyelinating diseases such as multiple sclerosis. However, both volume loss and white matter signal abnormalities are somewhat greater than typical for this age group. There may also be symmetric T2 hyperintensity in bilateral globi pallidi which is of unclear clinical significance. Following contrast administration, there are no focal areas of abnormal enhancement. No areas of restricted diffusion are present to suggest acute infarction. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. There are normal vascular flow voids at the base of the brain. IMPRESSION: 1. Volume loss and scattered white matter signal abnormalities are greater than typical for this age group. 2. White matter findings are distributed in a pattern atypical for primary demyelinating diseases such as multiple sclerosis. However, questionable symmetric signal abnormalities in the globi pallidi in conjunction with volume loss and white matter changes raise the possibility of toxic exposure, such as to carbon monoxide. Comparison to prior outside imaging would be helpful if available to check for progression of findings in the interval. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: A large bifrontal tumor has grown significantly in the interim, now measuring 5.1 x 6.0 x 4.8 cm in the right frontal lobe, with a smaller component in the left frontal lobe now with a new left frontal satellite nodule. This shows restricted diffusion. Perfusion images could not be performed due to problems with the patient's injection port. Surrounding FLAIR signal abnormality has progressed in the interim. Intrinsic T1 signal hyperintensity within the medial aspect of the left frontal mass, which blooms on GRE sequences is consistent with intratumoral petechial hemorrhage. There is no hydrocephalus, or herniation. There is evidence of prior right frontal craniotomy. The paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. The orbital contents are symmetric and intact. No suspicious calvarial lesions are identified. The visualized soft tissues of the head and face are without focal abnormality. The visualized vascular flow voids of the intracranial circulation are normal-appearing. IMPRESSION: 1. MARKED INTERVAL ENLARGEMENT OF BIFRONTAL MASSES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Post-surgical changes are present following resection of a large frontal meningioma, including left frontal craniotomy changes with overlying scalp swelling and subcutaneous emphysema. Blood products are seen in association with recent left frontal resection bed. There has been interval improvement in left to right midline shift after removal of a great majority of the previously seen large left frontal meningioma. However, there is residual enhancing tissue seen along the anterior and superior margins of the resection bed, particularly along the interhemispheric falx. A small area of restricted diffusion extending from the posterior margin of the resection cavity into the left frontal lobe is consistent with a small infarction. There are no new regions of abnormal signal intensity or enhancement to suggest additional disease. Ventricles and extra-axial fluid spaces remain appropriate in size and symmetric in configuration. The paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain appear patent. IMPRESSION: 1. Post-surgical changes are present following resection of a large frontal meningioma, notable for a small posterior pericavitary infarction. 2. Interval improvement in left to right midline shift after removal of a great majority of the previously seen large left frontal meningioma. However, there is residual enhancing tissue seen along the anterior and superior margins of the resection bed, particularly along the interhemispheric falx, consistent with residual tumor. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.
