input: FINDINGS: There are numerous hemispheric white matter lesions on axial T2 and FLAIR, many of which have a rather periventricular distribution. There is a larger periatrial right occipital lesion which demonstrates enhancement following contrast administration, likely a large active demyelinating lesion. There is enhancement of the right frontal opercular region as well. There is mild prominence of the ventricles, cisterns, and sulci for age. There are no extra-axial collections. There is no acute infarction or hemorrhage. MR Angiogram demonstrates no intracranial aneurysm dissection or flow-limiting stenosis. MR Venogram demonstrates a co-dominant venous system with mild flattening of the internal jugular veins immediately below the skull base, slightly worse on the right with slower flow. The more caudal aspects of the jugular venous system assume a more normal course and caliber. There is mild flattening of the brachiocephalic vein. The azygos vein is normal in appearance. There are prominent posterior suboccipital collateral venous structures. Incidental note is made of a large posterior nasopharyngeal Thornwaldt cyst. IMPRESSION: 1. ADVANCED HEMISPHERIC WHITE MATTER DISEASE, WITH ENHANCEMENT INVOLVING A RIGHT FRONTAL OPERCULAR AND RIGHT PERIATRIAL OCCIPITAL LESION, POSSIBLY REFLECTING ACTIVE DEMYELINATION. CLINICAL CORRELATION IS NECESSARY. 2. NORMAL MR VENOGRAM AND MR ANGIOGRAM OF THE BRAIN. 3. BILATERAL FLATTENING OF THE BILATERAL INTERNAL JUGULAR VEINS AT THE UPPER CERVICAL LEVEL, RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT. MILD FLATTENING IS ALSO NOTED IN THE MORE CAUDAL ASPECTS OF THE JUGULAR VENOUS SYSTEM, AS WELL AS INVOLVING THE BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN, DETAILED ABOVE. THE AZYGOS VEIN IS NORMAL IN APPEARANCE. 4. POSTERIOR NASOPHARYNGEAL THORNWALDT CYST. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no definite acute infarction or hydrocephalus. There is a multilobulated, ovoid 2.1-cm region of probable subacute hemorrhage in the right occipital lobe immediately cephalad to the calcarine sulcus. There is a paucity of surrounding vasogenic edema. There is no definite associated abnormal enhancement; however, assessment is somewhat difficult due to the intrinsically T1 hyperintense nature of the abnormality. There are no other definite acute hemorrhages. There are a small number of scattered, nonspecific white matter lesions bilaterally. There are no extraaxial collections. Midline structures are normal in appearance. The posterior fossa is unremarkable. MR angiogram demonstrates a normal course and caliber of the intracranial circulation without aneurysm, dissection, flow-limiting stenosis or gross vascular malformation. Major venous sinuses appear patent. IMPRESSION: 1. A 2-CM INTRAPARENCHYMAL HEMORRHAGE IN THE RIGHT OCCIPITAL LOBE IMMEDIATELY CEPHALAD TO THE CALCARINE SULCUS WITHOUT A DEFINITE ASSOCIATED UNDERLYING ENHANCING ABNORMALITY. ASSESSMENT WAS SOMEWHAT LIMITED DUE TO THE INTRINSICALLY T1 HYPERINTENSE NATURE OF THIS LIKELY SUBACUTE HEMORRHAGE. FAVOR HEMORRHAGE IN SETTING OF SOLITARY CAVERNOMA 2. MR ANGIOGRAM DEMONSTRATING NO DEFINITE ANEURYSM OR VASCULAR MALFORMATION IN THIS DISTRIBUTION. THE MAJOR VENOUS SINUSES APPEAR TO BE PATENT ON THIS NONVENOGRAPHIC STUDY. 3. FOLLOW-UP TO RESOLUTION TO EXCLUDE POSSIBILITY OF UNDERLYING OCCULT MASS COULD BE CONSIDERED IF CLINICALLY INDICATED. 4. SMALL NUMBER OF NONSPECIFIC WHITE MATTER LESIONS, WHICH COULD REPRESENT SMALL VESSEL ISCHEMIC CHANGE IN THIS AGE GROUP. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Scout localizer images demonstrate no large extracerebral mass. Stable diffuse infiltrative signal abnormality and cortical thickening is present in the right paramedian frontal lobe. No foci of significant contrast enhancement is seen within the signal abnormality. There is no increase CBV compared to the prior exams. There is no abnormal enhancement on postcontrast images. The orbits, paranasal sinuses, mastoid air cells, flow-voids at the skull base, and cervicomedullary junction are preserved. Post surgical changes are again appreciated to the right frontal bone. MR PERFUSION: Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality. Specifically, there is no elevated blood volume. ASL: Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality. IMPRESSION: STABLE INFILTRATIVE SIGNAL ABNORMALITY WITH CORTICAL THICKENING INVOLVING THE RIGHT PARAMEDIAN FRONTAL LOBE CONSISTENT WITH PROVIDED HISTORY DIFFUSE LOW-GRADE ASTROCYTOMA SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED

input: FINDINGS: MRI BRAIN: No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarcts identified. No midline shift or herniation is noted. There is severe, confluent and extensive regions of patchy signal abnormalities involving the regions of the medulla, pons, midbrain, bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles, and periaqueductal gray matter, which is grossly stable when compared with prior study. Some focal areas of minimally decreased in size from prior examination. However, the overall appearance is not significantly changed. Extensive confluent white matter lesions are identified, and somewhat perpendicular relation to the corpus callosum, the latter being markedly thinned along the undersurface. A few scattered lesions may be new, including a left periventricular lesion (see image # 19 of series 6), located near the corona radiata and measuring 7-mm in size. However, this is difficult to evaluate given the extensive number of lesions. The overall lesion burden is not significantly changed. Extensive confluent white matter signal abnormality is identified bilaterally. There is associated mild volume loss. A few subtle areas of possible enhancement are identified including involvement of the left middle cerebellar peduncle (see image # 7 of series 2), a region of the right periventricular white matter within the right temporal lobe (see image # 10 of series 2), and a tiny focal area of presumed enhancement within the right temporal lobe seen on image # 12 of series 2. However, some of these may represent pulsation artifact. There is mild prominence of the ventricles and sulci consistent with mild volume loss, greater than expected for patient age. Minimal mucosal thickening is identified within the right maxillary sinus, which may be congestive or inflammatory. The remainder of the visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE OR INFARCT IDENTIFIED. 4. NUMEROUS DIFFUSE PATCHY AREAS OF SIGNAL ABNORMALITY WITHIN THE CERVICAL, THORACIC CORD, AND CONUS WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT AT THIS TIME. input: FINDINGS: There is no acute infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, intra axial mass, extra-axial collection rule out brain herniation. The ventricles and sulci are appropriate in size and configuration. The orbits are grossly unremarkable. There is a left maxillary mucous retention cyst / polyp. The remainder of the visualized orbits and mastoid air cells are clear. There is a 12 x 6 mm signal abnormality overlying the left frontal lobe anteriorly which is dark on all sequences (series 6, image 28, series 11, image 25); this abnormality does not demonstrate enhancement and appears to be extra-axial but not arising from the overlying bone and may represent a large dural calcification. No abnormal enhancement is seen in the brain or in the extra-axial space. There is no associated edema in the left frontal lobe underlying this signal abnormality. In comparison to a prior study of December 2003, this signal abnormality appears overall stable (on the prior study it was best seen on series 4, image 17, series 9, image 16). IMPRESSION: 1. OVOID 12 X 6 MM NONENHANCING, DARK (ON ALL SEQUENCES) EXTRA-AXIAL LESION OVERLYING THE LEFT FRONTAL LOBE MAY REPRESENT A LARGE DURAL CALCIFICATION OR A SMALL CALCIFIED MENINGIOMA. THIS IS OVERALL STABLE IN COMPARISON TO THE PRIOR MRI OF 12/26/2003. THERE IS NO ASSOCIATED SUBCORTICAL EDEMA. 2. OUTSIDE CT IS NOT AVAILABLE FOR COMPARISON. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are interval post surgical changes following a right sided craniotomy for bypass grafting. A tiny subacute subdural hematoma overlies the right cerebral hemisphere. There are scattered foci of restricted diffusion within the right corona radiata and periventricular white matter, reflective of small infarcts, in an anterior borderzone distribution. There is faint enhancement within the right frontal infarct. A tiny cortical focus of restricted diffusion is also seen in the right post-central gyrus. There is no intracranial hemorrhage or midline shift. The ventricles and sulci are stable and appropriate in size and configuration. Partial bilateral mastoid effusions as well some mild left maxillary mucosal thickening may be congestive or inflammatory. On MRA, the extra-cranial, trans-cranial, and intra-cranial segments of the right superficial temporal artery is patent. The anastomosis is not imaged. Severe stenosis of the right carotid terminus and proximal M1 segment are stable, with associated slow flow. On ASL perfusion, slow flow to the right MCA territory is seen. Borderzone Tmax prolongation appears stable. However, there is slight improvement in the cortical Tmax delay (series 1241, image 17 vs. previously 2641, image 14). IMPRESSION: 1. POST-OPERATIVE CHANGES FOLLOWING A RIGHT SIDED CRANIOTOMY FOR BYPASS GRAFTING, WITH A TINY SUBACUTE SUBDURAL HEMATOMA OVERLYING THE RIGHT CEREBRAL HEMISPHERE. 2. NEW SCATTERED FOCI OF ACUTE AND SUBACUTE INFARCTS, IN THE RIGHT ANTERIOR BORDERZONE DISTRIBUTION. 3. PATENT EXTRA-CRANIAL, TRANS-CRANIAL, AND INTRA-CRANIAL SEGMENTS OF THE RIGHT SUPERFICIAL TEMPORAL ARTERY. THE ANASTOMOSIS IS NOT IMAGED. 4. STABLE HIGH GRADE STENOSIS OF THE RIGHT CAROTID TERMINUS AND PROXIMAL M1 SEGMENT OF THE RIGHT MCA. 5. STABLE TMAX PROLONGATION IN THE RIGHT BORDERZONES. HOWEVER, THERE IS SLIGHT IMPROVEMENT IN THE CORTICAL TMAX DELAY IN THE RIGHT HEMISPHERE. FINDINGS WERE COMMUNICATED TO Dr. Watson Charlee, ON 02/21/04 AT 12:30 P. M. BY joziel k md. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Ventricle size appears mildly increased with decreased pneumocephalus. No evidence of transependymal flow of CSF. Diffusion weighted images redemonstrate in bilateral infarcts predominantly involving the basal ganglia, including the right caudate head and putamen, left posterior limb of the internal capsule, and punctate infarct in the high right frontal lobe. Associated wallerian degeneration is again seen. No new infarct. Enhancement of these subacute infarcts following contrast administration is redemonstrated. No additional areas of abnormal postcontrast enhancement. No new mass-effect, midline shift or herniation. No extra-axial collections. The mastoid air cells, paranasal sinuses, calvarium and skull base are unremarkable. MR PERFUSION: Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality. MRA: Diffuse irregularity is seen of the intracranial vasculature, stable. Apparently worsened with irregularity of the bilateral M1 segments is likely technical in nature. The bilateral intracranial ICA's, MCA's, ACA's and the anterior communicating artery are otherwise patent and unremarkable. Bilateral vertebral arteries and posterior cerebral arteries are patent. The basilar artery is normal in course in caliber, without significant stenosis or flow gap is identified. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL INCREASE IN VENTRICULAR SIZE WITH DECREASED PNEUMOCEPHALUS. 2. EVOLVING BILATERAL BASAL GANGLIA AND RIGHT FRONTAL INFARCTS. NO NEW INFARCT IS APPRECIATED. 3. STABLE IRREGULARITY OF THE INTRACRANIAL VASCULATURE. APPARENT WORSENED INVOLVEMENT OF THE BILATERAL M1 SEGMENTS IS LIKELY TECHNICAL IN NATURE, ALTHOUGH THIS CAN BE CORRELATED WITH TRANSCRANIAL DOPPLER. 4. RESULTS DISCUSSED WITH THE NEUROLOGY SERVICE AT THE TIME OF INTERPRETATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: There is interval decrease of enhancing lesion in the right globus pallidus, currently measuring 9 mm compared to 13 mm on prior examination. There is hemorrhage adjacent to the enhancing lesion as well as along the previous biopsy tract. There is significant interval improvement of the adjacent T2 / FLAIR high signal white matter abnormality, interval decrease of mass effect and midline shift. There is no new disease within the brain. No acute infarction. The orbits appear to be intact. Visualized paranasal sinuses, middle ear and mastoid air cells are clear. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL IMPROVEMENT OF ENHANCING RIGHT GLOBUS PALLIDUS LESION AND ADJACENT WHITE MATTER SIGNAL ABNORMALITY. INTERVAL DECREASE OF MASS EFFECT AND MIDLINE SHIFT. ALL THESE FINDINGS ARE COMPATIBLE WITH LYMPHOMA, FOLLOWING STEROID TREATMENT. 2. NO NEW DISEASE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are postsurgical changes within the right temporal lobe with stable encephalomalacia, gliosis, and associated ex vacuo dilatation of the right temporal horn. A right superior cerebellar lesion measures 4 mm. It previously measured up to 6 mm. Additional metastatic lesions are all smaller in size. No new lesion is identified. Scattered nonenhancing signal abnormalities within the white matter are nonspecific and unchanged. The previously seen focus of edema within the right frontal lobe has also decreased. There is no intracranial hemorrhage or acute infarct. There is no mass effect or midline shift. A small, enhancing extraaxial mass along the posterior falx is stable and may reflect an incidental meningioma. No significant mucosal thickening or fluid is seen within the paranasal sinuses or mastoid air cells. The bone marrow is diffusely low in signal intensity, unchanged from the prior study. IMPRESSION: 1. DECREASE IN SIZE OF MULTIPLE SUPRA AND INFRATENTORIAL INTRACRANIAL METASTASES. NO DEFINITE NEW LESION IS IDENTIFIED. 2. DIFFUSELY LOW MARROW SIGNAL INTENSITY, SIMILAR TO THE PRIOR STUDY, CAN BE SEEN WITH CHRONIC ANEMIA, TREATMENT EFFECTS, OR DIFFUSE MARROW INFILTRATION. SUMMARY: 2 ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MR BRAIN: No acute intracranial hemorrhage or acute infarct. No focal brain parenchymal lesion or area of focal signal abnormality is present. No mass effect or midline shift. Ventricular size is within normal limits. No abnormal extra-axial fluid collections. No abnormal enhancement is seen. MRA / MRV HEAD AND NECK: The superior sagittal sinus, torcula, transverse and sigmoid sinuses are patent. There is moderate flattening at the superior aspect of the bilateral internal jugular veins, near the C1 / C2 articulation, with more significant flattening of the left internal jugular vein more inferiorly, at the mid C2 level (image 23 of series 13). The more inferior portions of the bilateral internal jugular veins are normal in caliber. The azygos vein is poorly visualized on the current examination.. Mild suboccipital collateral venous structures are identified the debated clinical significance. MR PERFUSION: Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality, as demonstrated on arterial spin labeling images. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF ACUTE INTRACRANIAL PATHOLOGY. 2. MODERATE FLATTENING OF THE SUPERIOR ASPECT OF THE BILATERAL INTERNAL JUGULAR VEINS, NEAR THE C1/2 ARTICULATION, WITH MORE SIGNIFICANT NARROWING OF THE LEFT INTERNAL JUGULAR VEIN, AT THE MID C2 LEVEL. THE REMAINDER THE BILATERAL INTERNAL JUGULAR VEINS ARE PATENT AND WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS. 3. NO PERFUSION ASYMMETRY IDENTIFIED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: superior bilateral internal jugular veins near C1/C2 articulation (location:present)
inferior left internal jugular vein at mid C2 level (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: MRI BRAIN: There is redemonstration of multiple foci of signal abnormality in the supratentorial white matter which do not enhance: These are overall stable in size and number in comparison to the prior study and some of them are better defined in comparison to the prior study which may be secondary to evolution of the lesions. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, infarction, intra-axial / extra-axial mass, extra-axial collection or brain herniation. The ventricles and sulci are appropriate in size and morphology. Visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. MRI CERVICAL SPINE: There is normal bony alignment of the cervical spine with normal bone marrow signal intensity. The cervical spine and cord is normal in caliber and contour without signal abnormality. MRI THORACIC SPINE: There is redemonstration of signal abnormality involving the spinal cord at the levels of T10 to T12 which is overall stable in comparison to the prior study. This does not demonstrate enhancement. No other areas of signal abnormality are seen in the thoracic spinal cord. There is redemonstration of left paracentral disc herniation causing compression of the spinal cord at the level of T8-T9 which is mild. IMPRESSION: 1. REDEMONSTRATION OF MULTIPLE, SUPRATENTORIAL WHITE MATTER SIGNAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE BRAIN WHICH DO NOT DEMONSTRATE ENHANCEMENT. THESE ARE OVERALL STABLE IN SIZE AND NUMBER IN COMPARISON TO THE PRIOR STUDY; SOME OF THEM ARE BETTER DEFINED ON THE PRESENT STUDY WHICH MAY BE SECONDARY TO DIFFERENCES IN TECHNIQUE OR LESION EVOLUTION. 2. NO SIGNAL ABNORMALITY WITHIN THE CERVICAL SPINAL CORD. 3. REDEMONSTRATION OF STABLE SIGNAL ABNORMALITY AT THE LEVEL OF T10 TO T12 VERTEBRAL BODIES IN THE THORACIC SPINAL CORD. NO NEW DISEASE IN THE THORACIC SPINAL CORD. 4. STABLE, LEFT PARACENTRAL DISC HERNIATION MILDLY COMPRESSING THE SPINAL CORD AT THE LEVEL OF T8-T9. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Scout localizer images demonstrate no large extracerebral mass. Mild degenerative changes are seen within the cervical spine. Postsurgical changes consistent with transphenoidal resection of pituitary tumor, and bilateral submucosal resection of the inferior turbinate, and polypectomy are present. The previously demonstrated lesion situated within the sellar, suprasellar region has been resected. There is no appreciable abnormal soft tissue seen within the sella. Normal pituitary tissue is seen. The infundibulum is midline. The optic chiasm and optic nerves are normal in appearance. The cavernous sinus and internal carotid arteries are normal in appearance. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, midline shift, or acute infarction. There are no focal parenchymal signal abnormalities present. Abnormal intra-axial or extra-axial fluid collections are not present. There is no abnormal enhancement on postcontrast images. A right maxillary sinus mucus retention cyst is present. The remaining paranasal sinuses are well aerated. The orbits, mastoid air cells, flow-voids at the skull base, and cervicomedullary junction are preserved. The calvarium demonstrates no abnormalities. IMPRESSION: 1. STATUS POST TRANSPHENOIDAL RESECTION OF SELLAR LESION WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF SIGNIFICANT RESIDUAL TUMOR. 2. RIGHT MAXILLARY SINUS MUCUS RETENTION CYSTS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION

MRA HEAD: No evidence of flow-limiting stenosis within the circle Willis. No evidence of cerebral aneurysm. Mild tortuosity of the left vertebral artery. SPECIFICALLY, NO ASYMMETRIC SIGNAL OR ENHANCEMENT WITHIN RIGHT TRIGEMINAL NERVE DEMONSTRATED. 2. UNREMARKABLE APPEARANCE OF THE ANTERIOR AND POSTERIOR CIRCULATIONS. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, or acute intracranial infarction. There is a right parietal approach ventricular catheter with tip in the midline in the expected location of the foramen of Monro; stable when compared to the prior study. IMPRESSION: 1. RE-DEMONSTRATION OF DYSMORPHIC BRAIN AND VENTRICLES WITH ATROPHY OF THE OPTIC CHIASM AND POSTERIOR FOSSA STRUCTURES. 2. UPPER CERVICAL SPINAL CANAL STENOSIS. 3. STABLE POSITION OF RIGHT PARIETAL APPROACH VENTRICULAR CATHETER WITH STABLE CONFIGURATION IN SIZE OF THE DYSMORPHIC VENTRICLES. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: BRAIN: Likely age-related atrophic changes are present within the brain. Diffusion imaging demonstrates areas of restricted diffusion within the left MCA territory including the insula as well as the left frontal lobe and anterior parietal lobe. Punctate areas of restricted diffusion are also present within the left frontal lobe superiorly as well as the right frontal lobe, the bilateral posterior watershed zones, and the left thalamus superiorly. There is slight enlargement of the left ventricle as compared to the right. There is no discrete mass or mass effect. No acute intracranial hemorrhage is identified. The extra-axial spaces are appropriate in size and relatively symmetric in configuration. INTRACRANIAL ANGIOGRAM: There is no flow-limiting occlusion or aneurysm of the circle of Willis. The bilateral posterior communicating arteries are not well visualized. There is irregularity of the cavernous and supraclinoid segments of the left internal carotid artery. The left MCA is diminutive and irregular. The right supraclinoid internal carotid artery is irregular. The right M1 and M2 segments of the MCA are diminutive. The bilateral posterior cerebral arteries are beaded in appearance, consistent with atherosclerosis. There is a left dominant vertebral artery. The basilar artery is tortuous. CONTRAST PERFUSION: Increased Tmax within the left MCA territory is present, with a clear region decreased CBV and CBF within the central infarct as well as regions preserved and / or increased in CBV in the periphery, suggestive of an evolving infarction. IMPRESSION: 1. THERE IS ACUTE INFARCTION WITHIN THE LEFT MCA TERRITORY INVOLVING THE INSULA AND PREDOMINANTLY THE POSTERIOR LEFT FRONTAL LOBE AS WELL AS ANTERIOR LEFT PARIETAL LOBE. PUNCTATE REGIONS OF POSTERIOR WATERSHED INFARCTIONS ARE PRESENT BILATERALLY AND THERE ARE ALSO PUNCTATE INFARCTIONS INVOLVING THE RIGHT FRONTAL LOBE AS WELL AS LEFT HIGH THALAMUS. PERFUSION IMAGING DEMONSTRATES INFARCTION WITHIN THE CENTER OF THE LEFT MCA TERRITORY WITH AN EVOLVING HALO OF TISSUE OF ABNORMAL PERFUSION. GIVEN THE MULTIFOCAL NATURE OF THESE INFARCTIONS AND LACK OF FOCAL VASCULAR ABNORMALITY, THERE MAY BE AN EMBOLIC SOURCE TO THESE FINDINGS. 2. THERE IS BILATERAL INTRACRANIAL VASCULAR DISEASE, WITHOUT FOCAL OCCLUSION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: VASCULATURE: The thoracic aorta and the great arteries appear intact. No dissection, aneurysm, or periaortic or mediastinal hematoma identified. While not a dedicated pulmonary embolism study, no filling defects are seen in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. CHEST: The central airways are intact. An endotracheal tube terminates approximately 4.5 cm above the carina. Secretions and / or debris are seen within the trachea and main bronchi. Ill-defined ground glass opacity nodules in the posterior aspects of bilateral upper lobes and right lower lobe likely reflect aspiration. Ill-defined patchy ground glass opacities in the right middle lobe may also represent aspiration, but infection is also a consideration. Dependent bibasilar consolidations likely represent atelectasis, but underlying aspiration is not excluded given debris in the airways. The heart size is within normal limits, without pericardial effusion. There is no adenopathy. Small epicardial lymph nodes are nonspecific. The thyroid gland appears normal. No pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or pneumomediastinum identified. Please refer to separately dictated report for abdomen and pelvis findings. MUSCULOSKELETAL: Old posterior right 11th and 12th rib fractures are noted. Old fracture deformity of the right mid clavicle is also noted. Deformity of the upper sternum also likely represents a healed fracture. No acute fracture identified. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC VASCULAR INJURY. 2. ILL-DEFINED GROUND GLASS OPACITIES IN THE POSTERIOR ASPECTS OF BILATERAL UPPER LOBES AND RIGHT LOWER LOBES LIKELY REPRESENT ASPIRATION GIVEN DISTRIBUTION AND DEBRIS / SECRETIONS IN THE AIRWAYS. DEPENDENT BIBASILAR CONSOLIDATIONS LIKELY REPRESENT ATELECTASIS, BUT SUPERIMPOSED ASPIRATION IS NOT EXCLUDED. 3. PATCHY RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE GROUND GLASS OPACITY MAY ALSO REPRESENT ASPIRATION, BUT INFECTION IS ALSO A CONSIDERATION. HEMORRHAGE IS CONSIDERED LESS LIKELY GIVEN THE LACK OF SURROUNDING SECONDARY FINDINGS OF TRAUMA. 4. PLEASE REFER TO SEPARATELY DICTATED REPORT FOR ABDOMEN AND PELVIS FINDINGS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Limited by respiratory motion. The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. Visualized portions of the aorta are normal in course and caliber. No filling defects seen within the central pulmonary arterial tree to suggest pulmonary embolism. The peripheral vessels cannot be effectively evaluated. There has been interval enlargement of multiple pulmonary nodules and wedge shaped peripheral areas of consolidation. No pneumothorax. Prominent hilar nodes are present, likely reactive. Evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. No suspicious bony lesions identified. Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO CENTRAL PULMONARY EMBOLUS. LIMITED EVALUATION OF THE PERIPHERAL VESSELS SECONDARY TO RESPIRATORY MOTION. 2. WORSENING BILATERAL LOCULATED PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH INCREASED MULTIFOCAL NODULES AND WEDGE SHAPED PERIPHERAL CONSOLIDATION COMPATIBLE WITH INFECTIOUS PROCESS, POSSIBLY SEPTIC EMBOLI. FUNGAL ETIOLOGIES SHOULD BE CONSIDERED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The thyroid gland is heterogeneous with bilateral low-density nodules. Evaluation of the venous structures reveals complete occlusion of the left internal jugular vein from the level of the hyoid bone inferiorly. There is a small remnant of the inferior jugular vein superiorly in the neck, seen best on series 4, image 10. At the junction of the left axillary and subclavian veins, there is an area of focal occlusion or high grade stenosis (series 4, image 25). However, the left brachiocephalic vein is occluded. The left carotid artery is medially displaced and is causing distortion of the oropharyngeal contour. There are multiple left anterior chest wall collateral vessels due to the venous occlusion described. The right distal internal jugular vein is also occluded at the level superior to the right clavicle (series 4, image 22). However, the right axillary and subclavian veins are patent. Within the right axillary vein in the axilla, there is a filling defect which may be a venous valve or focal stenosis (series 3, image 194). The right brachiocephalic vein is patent but diminutive. The superior vena cava and azygos veins are patent. Evaluation of the mediastinum reveals several prominent low-density lymph nodes; for example, there is a 14 mm right paratracheal lymph node as well as a 10 mm precarinal lymph node. These may be the result of engorgement of the lymph nodes due to venous obstruction. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. Heart size is within normal limits. The ascending aorta is mildly dilated measuring up to 41 mm in size. The right pulmonary artery is also mildly dilated measuring 25 mm in size. There are coronary artery calcifications. Regional osseous structures appear unremarkable. Evaluation of the upper abdomen reveals a cyst at the junction of the right and left lobes of the liver as well as two cysts within the upper poles of the left kidney. IMPRESSION: 1. OCCLUSION OF THE LEFT INTERNAL JUGULAR VEIN AS WELL AS LEFT BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN. THERE IS A FOCAL OCCLUSION OR HIGH-GRADE STENOSIS AT THE JUNCTION OF THE LEFT AXILLARY AND LEFT SUBCLAVIAN VEINS. THERE ARE NUMEROUS ASSOCIATED LEFT CHEST WALL COLLATERALS. 2. OCCLUSION OF THE INFERIOR RIGHT INTERNAL JUGULAR VEIN AT THE LEVEL OF THE CLAVICLE. HOWEVER, THE RIGHT SUBCLAVIAN AND BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEINS ARE PATENT. THE SUPERIOR VENA CAVA IS ALSO PATENT. 3. MILD ANEURYSMAL DILATATION OF THE ASCENDING THORACIC AORTA TO 41 MM. 4. MULTIPLE PROMINENT LOW-DENSITY MEDIASTINAL LYMPH NODES. THESE COULD BE ENGORGED LYMPH NODES DUE TO THE ABOVE-MENTIONED VENOUS OBSTRUCTION. 5. SEVERAL LOW-DENSITY LESIONS WITHIN THE THYROID GLAND. SUMMARY: 4 POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: left internal jugular vein inferior to hyoid bone (location:present)
right distal internal jugular vein superior to right clavicle (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: There is an adequate contrast bolus for opacification of the pulmonary arteries. However, there is some respiratory motion limiting the evaluation of the subsegmental pulmonary arteries. Even with this limitation, bilateral lower lobe segmental pulmonary emboli are appreciated, specifically, emboli are appreciated in the left posterior basal segment as well as the right posterolateral basal segment. Additionally, there is ground-glass opacity in the left lower lobe posterior basal segment that is consistent with a Hampton hump / postembolic hemorrhage. Additional bibasilar consolidation could simply represent atelectasis, however, in light of the pulmonary emboli, some component of infarction is not excluded. Mild paraseptal emphysematous changes are appreciated in the apices. No significant pleural effusions. Normal heart size, with no pericardial effusion. There is some fatty change of the mid anterior septum in a subendocardial location, this could possibly be related to a prior infarct / scarring. Calcific coronary artery disease is appreciated, with the possible appearance of a stent in the left anterior descending artery, however, this is somewhat motion limited. Correlate with patient history. The trachea and central bronchi appear patent. Multiple prominent mediastinal lymph nodes, with the largest measuring 1 cm in short-axis diameter in a right paratracheal location, these are of unclear significance and could be reactive in nature. A moderate hiatal hernia is present. Evaluation of the visualized osseous structures demonstrates no acute abnormalities. Evaluation of the abdomen and pelvis will be provided in a separate report. IMPRESSION: 1. BILATERAL LOWER LOBE SEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLI, WITH SOME POSTEMBOLIC HEMORRHAGE IN THE LEFT LOWER LOBE. THE BIBASILAR CONSOLIDATION COULD BE DUE TO ATELECTASIS, HOWEVER, SOME COMPONENT OF INFARCTION IS NOT EXCLUDED. 2. FATTY CHANGE OF THE MID ANTERIOR CARDIAC SEPTUM IN A SUBENDOCARDIAL LOCATION IN CONJUNCTION WITH CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE. CANNOT EXCLUDE THIS BEING RELATED TO A PRIOR INFARCT / SCARRING. 3. MILD EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGES. 4. MODERATE HIATAL HERNIA. RESULTS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Dr. Rebekah Hunold AT APPROXIMATELY 2144 HOURS ON 4-11-2011 BY Evie Lucero MD, THE RADIOLOGY RESIDENT ON CALL. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

related: left lower lobe posterior basal segment (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: CHEST: Endotracheal tube lies 8 mm above the carina. A nasogastric tube is seen extending into the body of the stomach. The thoracic vasculature is unremarkable with no evidence of vascular injury. There is no discrete abnormality in the mediastinum with normal appearing thymus and no visible hemorrhage. No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thyroid gland is unremarkable and the central airways are patent. The pulmonary parenchyma is unremarkable for technique with no evidence of parenchymal mass or contusion and no pneumothorax. No pleural or pericardial effusion. ABDOMEN AND PELVIS: Within the left lateral hepatic lobe there is a mildly lobulated hypervascular mass measuring 2.3 x 2.1 cm which becomes isoattenuating on delayed imaging. No additional liver lesions are identified, with incidental focal fat adjacent to the falciform ligament. The hepatic vasculature is patent and there is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. There is moderate periportal edema and the IVC is prominent, findings likely related to volume resuscitation. There is no discrete free fluid in the abdomen or pelvis and no free intraperitoneal air. The spleen, kidneys, adrenal glands and pancreas are grossly unremarkable without evidence of solid organ injury. The stomach is mild to moderately distended but the bowel is otherwise grossly unremarkable. Within the pelvis the urinary bladder is mild to moderately distended and thin-walled with an indwelling Foley catheter noted. The uterus contains a 13 x 22 mm subserosal fundal fibroid and appears otherwise unremarkable. The adnexa are grossly unremarkable though not well visualized due to paucity of pelvic fat. The vasculature of the abdomen and pelvis is unremarkable with no evidence of injury and no aneurysmal dilatation. MUSCULOSKELETAL: The bones are unremarkable with no evidence of fracture. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC INJURY TO THE CHEST, ABDOMEN OR PELVIS. 2.2. 3 CM HYPERVASCULAR MASS SEEN IN THE LEFT HEPATIC LOBE, DIFFERENTIAL FOR WHICH INCLUDES BENIGN AND MALIGNANT HYPERVASCULAR TUMORS. IF PATIENT IS ON ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILLS, FINDINGS ARE MOST LIKELY TO REPRESENT AN ADENOMA. OTHERWISE, THE DIFFERENTIAL WOULD INCLUDE LESS LIKELY FOCAL NODULAR HYPERPLASIA, ATYPICAL FLASH FILL HEMANGIOMA, OR OTHER TUMORS. FURTHER EVALUATION WITH MRI COULD BE CONSIDERED IF CLINICALLY INDICATED. FINDINGS WERE TEXT-PAGED TO TRAUMA INTERN AT 12:05 PM ON 2/28/2016 SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no evidence for a pulmonary arterial filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. No adenopathy is seen in the mediastinum, hila, or axilla. The thyroid gland appears enlarged but no discrete thyroid nodule is seen. There is diffuse severe bilateral centrilobular emphysema. Mild atelectasis is present in the lingula. Mild bibasilar subsegmental atelectasis is also noted. There is no lung consolidation. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Biapical scarring is noted. There is mild bronchial wall thickening, which is nonspecific and may be chronic. Small metallic foreign bodies in the left chest wall, suggestive of bullet fragments. There are moderate degenerative changes in the thoracic spine. No acute osseous abnormality is seen. There is partial visualization of small presumed cyst in the right kidney measuring up to 2.2 cm (series 3, image one). There is an ill-defined rounded area of hyperattenuation in the superior pole of the left kidney, incompletely visualized (series 3, image 6). IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE FOR PULMONARY EMBOLISM OR OTHER ACUTE PROCESS IN THE CHEST. 2. SEVERE CENTRILOBULAR EMPHYSEMA. 3. PARTIAL VISUALIZATION OF A SUBTLE, ROUNDED HYPERDENSE VERSUS HYPERENHANCING LESION IN THE LEFT RENAL UPPER POLE. RECOMMEND CORRELATION WITH RENAL ULTRASOUND. 4. THYROMEGALY. THIS CAN ALSO BE FURTHER ASSESSED WITH ULTRASOUND IF CLINICALLY WARRANTED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: VASCULATURE: The thoracic aorta and the great arteries appear intact. No dissection, aneurysm, periaortic hematoma, or mediastinal hematoma identified. While not a dedicated pulmonary embolism study, no filling defects are seen in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. CHEST: There is moderate bilateral dependent atelectasis. No pneumothorax is identified. No convincing pulmonary contusion is identified. The heart size is within normal limits. There are extensive coronary artery calcifications. The central airways are clear. There is no mediastinal, hilar, supraclavicular, or axillary adenopathy. The thyroid gland appears normal. Large combined paraesophageal and hiatal hernia is present. No pleural or pericardial effusion seen. There is a small rind of extra pleural hematoma and air within the right anterior third and fourth intercostal space measuring 8 mm in thickness and 4 cm in width. Evaluation of the abdomen dictated in a separate report. MUSCULOSKELETAL: Several right-sided rib fractures are identified. These include nondisplaced to minimally displaced fractures of the anterior third through sixth ribs. There are additional fractures of the posterolateral third through ninth ribs. A linear lucency seen at the level of the right greater tuberosity is incompletely evaluated possibly additional fracture. No suspicious bony lesions identified. Soft tissues are normal in appearance. IMPRESSION: 1. MULTIPLE RIGHT-SIDED RIB FRACTURES AS DESCRIBED ABOVE WITH A SMALL FOCUS ANTERIORLY OF EXTRAPLEURAL HEMATOMA AND AIR. 2. LINEAR LUCENCY TO THE LEVEL OF THE RIGHT GREATER TUBEROSITY SUGGESTIVE OF FRACTURE, CORRELATION WITH PLAIN FILMS IS RECOMMENDED. 3. OTHERWISE, NO EVIDENCE FOR INTRATHORACIC INJURY. 4. EXTENSIVE CORONARY ARTERY CALCIFICATION, CORRELATE WITH CARDIAC RISK FACTORS. 5. LARGE COMBINED PARAESOPHAGEAL AND SLIDING HIATAL HERNIA. 6. ABDOMINAL FINDINGS TO BE DESCRIBED IN SEPARATE REPORT. 7. THESE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Menk Graham MD OF THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ON 5/21/2005 AT 1347 HRS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. There is subsegmental right upper lobe linear atelectasis. There is no pleural effusion. A large, heterogeneous mass is seen within the left upper quadrant arising measuring 13 x 14.6 cm in axial dimension. It was seen on prior exam to be arising from the left kidney most compatible with a large renal cell carcinoma. The spleen also appears enlarged up to 14 cm in AP dimension. A well-circumscribed enhancing focus within the posterior aspect of the spleen measures 19-mm and is likely to represent a splenic hemangioma. Scattered bone islands are noted. No suspicious osseous lesion is detected. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE FOR PULMONARY EMBOLUS. 2. PARTIAL VISUALIZATION OF LARGE LEFT UPPER QUADRANT MASS LIKELY TO REPRESENT A LARGE RENAL CELL CARCINOMA. 3. MILD SPLENOMEGALY AND SPLENIC HEMANGIOMA. 4. THESE FINDINGS WERE RELAYED VIA PACS PRELIMINARY NOTE THAT pa-c obrien ON 06/25/2016 at 1821 HRS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are postsurgical changes of aortic coarctation repair by subclavian flap aortoplasty, with no significant focal stenosis of the thoracic aorta. The mid ascending thoracic aorta measures 20 mm. There is a relatively hypoplastic segment of the aortic arch between the brachiocephalic and left carotid artery takeoffs, where the aorta measures 12 mm in diameter. The proximal descending thoracic aorta measures 14 mm. The left subclavian artery origin is occluded at its origin. There is filling of the more distal left subclavian artery via prominent intercostal collaterals. There is incidental note of an early takeoff of a right ventricle arterial branch off the right coronary artery. The left coronary artery appears to be dominant and is widely patent. There is early bifurcation of the left coronary artery. The aortic valve is bicuspid (Sievers Type I) and there is prolapse of the combined right and left cusp leaflet. There is associated marked dilation of the left ventricle. The right ventricle also appears enlarged. The lungs are clear. There is no pericardial or pleural effusion. The visualized upper abdomen appears normal. There are postsurgical changes of the left posterior chest wall, including partial fusion of some of the left sided ribs. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: c058278556 Diameters in mm Major Minor Aorta at arch 13.40 12.40 Aorta at-Diasyst Rt pulmonary art 18.30 16.30 Proximal descending thoracic aorta 15.30 13.60 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. POSTSURGICAL CHANGES OF AORTIC COARCTATION REPAIR BY SUBCLAVIAN FLAP AORTOPLASTY. OVERALL SOMEWHAT HYPOPLASTIC AORTIC ARCH WITH NO SIGNIFICANT FOCAL NARROWING. 2. BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE (SIEVERS TYPE 1, R-L, I) WITH EVIDENCE OF PROLAPSE OF THE FUSED R / L CUSPS AND MARKED LEFT VENTRICULAR DILATATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Evaluation of the mediastinum demonstrates surgical clips in the thyroid bed, compatible with previous thyroidectomy. Small amount of flat, non-confluent fibrofatty soft tissue is seen in the prevascular space, presumed to represent some residual thymic tissue. Heart size is within normal limits with no pericardial effusions. No significant axillary, mediastinal or hilar adenopathy is seen. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates a 4 mm left lower lobe nodule along the major fissure, similar in appearance to the exams on 11/20/2003 and 12-28-1997 accounting for differences in technique. No focal lung opacities or pleural effusions are seen. Limited evaluation of some of the upper abdominal structures is unremarkable. Evaluation of the visualized osseous structures demonstrates no suspicious-appearing lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

There is no pulmonary embolus in the main, left or right, or lobar branches of the pulmonary artery. Segmental and subsegmental pulmonary emboli cannot be excluded on this exam. There is 3-vessel coronary artery disease. The heart is within normal limits for size. There is no pericardial effusion. There is a left-sided 3-vessel aortic arch. There is atherosclerotic disease of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta with no aneurysmal dilatation. There are bilateral small pleural effusions greater on the right side than the left with associated atelectasis. There is a small area of non-calcified pleural thickening anteriorly along the anterior upper right hemithorax (Se 3 Im 95). There is no focal area of nodularity or consolidation. The trachea and mainstem bronchi are patent. There are prominent sub-carinal mediastinal lymph nodes measuring up to 10 mm short axis (Se 3 Im 75). No hilar or axillary adenopathy. The thyroid gland is unremarkable. Limited images of the upper abdomen are unremarkable. 4 mm sclerotic lesion in the right seventh rib most in keeping with a tiny bone island. Multilevel degenerative changes of the spine. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: 3127828090 Diameters in mm Major Minor Ascending aorta 44.00 39.10 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. input: FINDINGS: Postoperative changes are noted with median sternotomy wires and a small amount of fluid and gas in the mediastinum with the most prominent region in the lower substernal area measuring approximately 2.6 x 2.2 cm (5, 230). There may be minimal rim enhancement in this area, which could reflect normal expected findings of postoperative hematoma. The aortic root is unremarkable with surgical material seen in the region of the noncoronary cusps likely reflecting site of sinus of Valsalva repair. There is no evidence of residual or recurrent aneurysm or leak. The remainder of the aorta appears unremarkable. The right and left coronary arteries and their respective cusps demonstrate normal anatomic appearance with a right dominant coronary system. The thoracic aorta is unremarkable throughout its course with the exception of a few scattered atherosclerotic calcifications. There is no aneurysmal dilatation and no dissection. No large central or segmental pulmonary embolism. There is evidence of some hypertrophy of the left ventricular wall which can be seen in the setting of hypertension. The heart is otherwise unremarkable. A bovine arch is incidentally noted. There is no axillary lymphadenopathy with multiple scattered benign-appearing lymph nodes with fatty hila. There are multiple mediastinal lymph nodes measuring up to 10 mm in short axis all of which have normal expected fatty hila and are presumably reactive. There is small amount of pericardial fluid and no pleural effusion. Within the lung parenchyma, there is focal atelectasis of the left lower lobe; the lungs are otherwise clear. UPPER ABDOMEN: Visualized upper abdominal structures are grossly unremarkable. MUSCULOSKELETAL: Changes related to recent sternotomy. Otherwise, no evidence of acute or suspicious bony abnormality. IMPRESSION: 1. STATUS POST SINUS OF VALSALVA ANEURYSM REPAIR WITH NO EVIDENCE OF LEAK OR RESIDUAL ANEURYSM. NORMAL APPEARANCE OF THE ASCENDING AORTA. 2. SMALL AMOUNT OF FLUID AND GAS WITHIN THE MEDIASTINUM RELATED TO RECENT SURGERY. 3. SUGGESTION OF HYPERTROPHY OF THE LEFT VENTRICLE WHICH CAN BE SEEN IN THE SETTING OF HYPERTENSION. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: VASCULAR: There is no evidence of pulmonary emboli. There is redemonstration of focal stenosis of the origin of the pulmonary artery to the posterior segment of the right upper lobe. In the left lower lobe, there is slight irregularity of one pulmonary artery branch on image 125, series 2, but this is most likely due to motion artifact. CHEST AND MEDIASTINUM: There is unchanged bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. There is heterogeneous attenuation within the lungs that is unchanged compared to the prior. This is again an expiratory scan and some of this may be secondary to air trapping and atelectasis. Redemonstration of enlarged pulmonary artery measuring 30 mm in diameter. This is consistent with the patient's history of pulmonary hypertension. ABDOMEN: The visualized upper abdominal structures are not fully evaluated in this arterial phase of contrast enhancement, but appear unremarkable. BONES: No lytic or sclerotic lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLI. REDEMONSTRATION OF BILATERAL HILAR ADENOPATHY AND HETEROGENEOUS ATTENUATION WITHIN THE LUNGS THAT COULD BE SECONDARY TO AIR TRAPPING BUT IS OVERALL UNCHANGED IN APPEARANCE COMPARED TO THE PRIOR STUDY. 2. REDEMONSTRATION OF ENLARGED MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY MEASURING 30 MM IN DIAMETER. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Right anterior chest wall dual lead cardiac pacemaker in place. The right ventricular lead penetrates the right ventricular free wall and extends through the pericardium with the tip in the pericardial fat. No evidence of contrast extravasation into the surrounding soft tissues or pericardial sac. Along the proximal portions of the pacer lead, at the junction of the brachiocephalic vein, there is a large area of low density consistent with thrombus measuring 14 x 9 mm. In addition, within the left main pulmonary artery there is an area of low density measuring approximately 2.6 x 1 cm at the level of the branch point of the upper lobe pulmonary artery consistent with pulmonary embolism, new compared to the prior study of 11/11/2018. No evidence of pulmonary embolism in the right lung. Large bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right. Complete consolidation of the left lower lobe with partial consolidation of the right lower lobe and left lingula. Areas of low density within the right lower lobe, left lower lobe and left lingula most consistent with infarction. Within the lateral left lower lobe and inferior segment of the left lingula there are foci of air suggesting lung necrosis. No evidence of pleural enhancement to suggest empyema. No mass lesion seen within the lungs. Centrilobular emphysema noted primarily in the lung apices. Compressive atelectasis at the lung bases. The heart is enlarged. Small pericardial effusion with mild pericardial enhancement which may be reactive versus pericarditis. On dynamic phase images there is flattening of the septum in systole suggesting increased right heart pressures. Three vessel coronary artery disease noted. Calcification within the anterolateral and posteromedial papillary muscles suggesting old infarct. Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. No gross abnormalities of the upper abdomen. Surgical changes in the left chest likely representing prior lumpectomy. IMPRESSION: 1. LEFT MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY EMBOLISM AS DESCRIBED WITH AREAS OF LUNG INFARCTION AND NECROSIS IN THE LEFT LINGULA AND LEFT LOWER LOBE, INFECTION CANNOT BE EXCLUDED. 2. THROMBUS ALONG THE PROXIMAL PACER LEADS AT THE REGION OF THE BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN. 3. RIGHT VENTRICULAR LEAD PENETRATION IN TO THE PERICARDIAL FAT BY APPROXIMATELY 1 CM, NO EVIDENCE OF ACTIVE CONTRAST EXTRAVASATION. 4. SMALL PERICARDIAL EFFUSION WITH MILD ENHANCEMENT OF THE PERICARDIUM WHICH MAY REPRESENT REACTIVE CHANGES VERSUS PERICARDITIS. 5. LOW DENSITY WITHIN THE CONSOLIDATED LUNG IN THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE SUGGESTING INFARCTION, NO EVIDENCE OF NECROSIS WITHIN THE RIGHT LUNG. 6. LARGE BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS, LEFT GREATER THAN RIGHT, NO EVIDENCE OF ENHANCEMENT TO SUGGEST EMPYEMA. FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Mcbrine Sarai AT 1645 HOURS ON 3/19/2020. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

related: proximal portions of the pacer lead (location:present)
branch point of the upper lobe pulmonary artery (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: No pulmonary embolus is identified. The proximal ascending aorta and main pulmonary artery are of normal caliber. The heart is of normal size with no significant pericardial effusion. The esophagus, trachea, and visualized portions of the thyroid gland are within normal limits. Redemonstration of multiple enlarged mediastinal, right hilar, and left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, which appears relatively unchanged compared to previous examination of 3-7-07. Since prior examination, there has been interval placement of a right-sided chest tube with interval decrease in the large right pleural effusion. There is residual nodular pleural thickening and complexloculated pleural fluid. There is improved aeration of the right lung. There is persistent right lower lobe atelectasis and / or consolidation. Redemonstration of a complex heterogeneous mass in the right upper lobe with areas of central necrosis, which measures approximately 10.6 x 8.5 x 9.5 cm. Again seen is invasion of the right anterior first rib, right upper chest wall with extension into the right axilla. There is persistent encasement of the right subclavian artery as well as medial invasion into the mediastinum, with persistent narrowing of the right upper lobe bronchus and right upper lobe pulmonary artery. - "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.

Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: Pulmonary arterial system is well opacified and exhibits no intraluminal filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. Mild inflow / mixing artifact is identified within left lower lobe subsegmental pulmonary arteries. The thoracic aorta appears normal. The pulmonary artery is a enlarged, measuring approximately 34 mm in diameter, which may be compatible with a component of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion. There is mild thickening of the esophagus, compatible to patient's stated history of esophageal adenocarcinoma. Multiple prominent mediastinal and paraesophageal lymph nodes are identified (series 4, image 36), measuring up to 24 x 17 mm diameter, compatible with metastatic spread of disease. Many of these lymph nodes have decreased in size compared to the prior examination dated January 1st, 2004. A small 2-mm groundglass nodule is identified in the right middle lobe (series 2, image 180), which is nonspecific in appearance and may be infectious or post inflammatory in nature. Atelectatic changes are identified at the bilateral lung bases. The bilateral lungs are otherwise clear. No pleural effusion. No aggressive osseous lesion is identified. Please refer to the patient's dedicated abdominal imaging for abdominal and pelvic findings. Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLI, AS CLINICALLY QUERIED. 2. SEQUELA OF METASTATIC ESOPHAGEAL CANCER, WITH DECREASED SIZE OF INTRATHORACIC LYMPH NODES COMPARED TO PRIOR EXAMINATION DATED 01/01/2004.3. PLEASE REFER TO SIMULTANEOUSLY OBTAINED DEDICATED ABDOMINAL / PELVIS IMAGING FOR ABDOMINAL / PELVIS FINDINGS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is a very large heterogeneous anterior mediastinal soft tissue mass with focal areas that are very low in attenuation suggestive of necrosis. This measures approximately 10.1 x 5.1 cm in maximum transverse dimensions. This abuts the aorta and pulmonary artery but there is no frank evidence of vascular invasion. There is some associated mass effect on the adjacent SVC which remains widely patent. Similarly this abuts the anterior chest wall and sternum without clear evidence of invasion. Additionally there are multiple irregular soft tissue nodules throughout the right axilla. There is a conglomerate asymmetric soft tissue mass in the apex of the right axilla best seen on series 4, image 34. This measures approximately 4.1 x 2.8 cm in size. Both the nodules and this conglomerate mass are markedly hypervascular. Additionally there is abnormal hypervascularity in the regional musculature including the latissimus dorsi and serratus anterior. These findings are concerning for hypervascular neoplasm. Given the anterior mediastinal mass this could either represent hypervascular nodal metastases in the setting of an anterior mediastinal germ cell tumor, thymic carcinoma or carcinoid, or potentially a thyroid carcinoma. Alternatively the anterior mediastinal mass could represent a lymphoma and these findings in the axilla would then represent additional nodal and extranodal lymphomatous involvement. The anterior mediastinal lesion would be amenable to a CT-guided percutaneous biopsy. There is compression atelectasis of the right upper lobe and portions of the right middle lobe that are adjacent to the mass. Otherwise the lungs are unremarkable. There is no focal consolidation. There is no edema. There are no pleural effusions. The regional osseous structures are unremarkable. There is normal opacification of the pulmonary arteries. There is no PE. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber and contour without evidence of aneurysm or dissection. IMPRESSION: 1. A 10.1 X 5.1 CM HETEROGENEOUSLY ENHANCING ANTERIOR MEDIASTINAL MASS. ADDITIONALLY THERE ARE MULTIPLE HYPERVASCULAR AXILLARY LYMPH NODES, A HYPERVASCULAR CONFLUENT SOFT TISSUE MASS IN THE HIGH RIGHT AXILLA AND ADDITIONAL AREAS OF HYPERVASCULARITY IN THE REGIONAL MUSCULATURE AS DESCRIBED. THE DIFFERENTIAL WOULD INCLUDE LYMPHOMA, THYMOMA / THYMIC CARCINOMA OR CARCINOID, THYROID CANCER, OR POSSIBLY GERM CELL TUMOR. THEREFORE, THE FINDINGS IN THE AXILLA COULD EITHER REPRESENT METASTATIC DISEASE FROM THE PRIMARY ANTERIOR MEDIASTINAL LESION OR ADDITIONAL NODAL AND EXTRANODAL INVOLVEMENT OF LYMPHOMA.. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF A THORACIC AORTIC ANEURYSM OR DISSECTION AS QUERIED. 3. THESE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Elise Wheeler PA IN THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ON 2003, January 21st AT 2314 HOURS. Elise Wheeler PA WAS CONTACTED BY THE ON CALL RADIOLOGY RESIDENT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: portions of the right middle lobe (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: There is no supraclavicular, axillary, hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. There is a right internal jugular central venous catheter with tip in the superior vena cava. Epicardial pacing wires are in place. There is a mediastinal drain. Postsurgical changes are seen status post valve sparing aortic root and hemiarch repair. Graft material is seen extending from the annulus proximally through the ascending aorta and proximal arch with hemiarch repair. The annulus appears normal. The coronary arteries have been reimplanted, and the coronary buttons appear normal. There is no evidence of contrast extravasation. There is no evidence of pseudoaneurysm. There is no evidence of dissection. The aortic graft measures 3.4 cm at the neosinuses. The graft lumen measures 2.8 cm in the ascending aorta. Postoperative hematoma is seen circumferentially around the ascending aorta and retrosternally. There is no evidence of rim-enhancing fluid collection to suggest abscess. The pulmonary artery appears normal without evidence of enlargement. There is no evidence of pulmonary embolism. The heart size is normal. There is a small amount of pericardial fluid. There are new small bilateral pleural effusions, right greater than left. There is minimal atelectasis at the right base. There are several calcified and noncalcified lung nodules, which were detailed on the prior examination. Limited examination of the upper abdomen partially demonstrates the hypervascular right renal mass, which was seen and measured 4.8 cm on the prior examination. Regional osseous are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. POSTOPERATIVE CHANGES STATUS POST TYRONE DAVID PROCEDURE FOR AORTIC ROOT AND ASCENDING AORTIC ANEURYSM. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF ANY POSTOPERATIVE COMPLICATION. SPECIFICALLY, THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF LEAK, PSEUDOANEURYSM OR DISSECTION. 2. REDEMONSTRATION OF A LARGE ENHANCING RIGHT KIDNEY MASS, WHICH IS HIGHLY CONCERNING FOR RENAL CELL CARCINOMA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

No mosaic attenuation. There is no pleural effusion. The central airways are patent. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. Multilevel degenerative changes are seen in the thoracic spine. IMPRESSION: 1. SUBPLEURAL RETICULATION WITH ASSOCIATED MILD GROUND GLASS AND MILD TRACTION BRONCHIECTASIS WITH A BASILAR PREDOMINANCE. FINDINGS ARE COMPATIBLE WITH A FIBROTIC NSIP PATTERN LIKELY REPRESENT DERMATOMYOSITIS ASSOCIATED ILD. NO HONEYCOMBING TO SUGGEST DEFINITE UIP. input: FINDINGS: There are interval postoperative changes of composite valve graft replacement of the proximal aortic valve and ascending aorta. There is an anastomosis of the distal ascending aorta. There is mediastinal hematoma and gas demonstrated. There is no extraluminal contrast to suggest postoperative complication. Right and left main coronary arteries arise from their respective cusps. This study was not targeted for the evaluation of coronary arteries. No hemodynamically significant stenosis is demonstrated. The patient demonstrates right dominant coronary branch anatomy. The patient demonstrates no focal wall motion abnormality on cine images. The prosthetic aortic valve demonstrates unremarkable appearance on cine images. Again demonstrated is a right aortic arch with aberrant left subclavian artery. A calcification in the AP window is again demonstrated and may reflect calcification of the ligamentum arteriosum. There is contour deformity and displacement of the trachea adjacent to the right arch, unchanged from the prior study. The descending thoracic aorta is on the right. There are large right greater than left pleural effusions. There is a small pericardial effusion. The lungs are clear of consolidation. There is bibasilar dependent atelectasis. There is a tiny pulmonary embolism noted within a subsegmental artery to the right lower lobe. There is a tiny filling defect within the right internal jugular vein in the expected course of a vascular catheter. This most likely represents tiny nonocclusive thrombus. Evaluation of the upper abdomen is limited by scope and timing of contrast administration. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. Osseous structures demonstrate sternotomy with wires. IMPRESSION: 1. POSTOPERATIVE CHANGES OF COMPOSITE VALVE GRAFT REPLACEMENT OF THE AORTIC VALVE AND ASCENDING AORTA, AS DESCRIBED. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF ACUTE POSTOPERATIVE COMPLICATION. 2. TINY SUBSEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLISM WITHIN THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE. 3. MODERATE TO LARGE BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS AND SMALL PERICARDIAL EFFUSION. 4. RIGHT SIDED ARCH WITH ABERRANT LEFT SUBCLAVIAN ARTERY AND RESULTANT DISPLACEMENT OF THE TRACHEA ARE AGAIN DEMONSTRATED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

instruction:

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: The ET tube is 1.2 cm above the carina. The NG tube terminates in the stomach. There is a right sided chest tube terminating posteriorly in the right mid chest. There is a right internal jugular central venous catheter terminating in the SVC. There is a small to moderate loculated right anterior pneumothorax. And there is a moderate sized right hemo-pneumothorax. The density is about 40 Hounsfield units and does not increase postcontrast. On the 3 phases of the CT chest that are obtained there is no gross extravasation of contrast is seen. The high density is seen inferiorly and posteriorly that stays the same density on all the sequences, suggesting clotted blood. There is a complete consolidation of the right lung. There are features of drowned lung, fluid filled lung. There is scattered patchy ground glass opacities with consolidation in the left lower lobe and lingula likely secondary to multifocal pneumonia. The previously seen right middle lobe mass is again seen but less well due to consolidation of the entire right lung. The thyroid gland appears grossly unremarkable. The heart chambers are not dilated. No pericardial effusion is appreciated. The pulmonary artery is well opacified. No pulmonary embolism is seen. There is no mediastinal, hilar, axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy based on CT size criteria. Limited visualization of the upper abdomen is non concerning in appearance. Osseous structures within the field of view are unremarkable. Addendum Begins 3D reformations consisting of curved and multiplanar reformations, maximum intensity projections, and volume rendered images were performed on an independent workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. ET TUBE, NG TUBE, A RIGHT-SIDED CHEST TUBE, A RIGHT IJV CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETER ARE IN POSITIONS. 2. RIGHT MODERATE HEMO-PNEUMOTHORAX. 3. NO ACTIVE EXTRAVASATION OF CONTRAST IS SEEN. 4. MASSIVE CONSOLIDATION OF THE RIGHT LUNG WITH DROWNED LUNG. THERE IS PATCHY CONSOLIDATION IN THE LEFT LUNG. THIS IS LIKELY SECONDARY TO MULTIFOCAL PNEUMONIA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.
