input: FINDINGS: The soft tissues of the lower neck are unremarkable. There is a 2-mm hypodensity in the left thyroid gland, which represent a lymph node or cyst. Correlate with physical exam, and if clinically appropriate, ultrasound may be considered for further evaluation. No pulmonary embolus is identified. Evaluation of the lung bases is mildly suboptimal due to respiratory motion, and small subsegmental pulmonary emboli may be secured at the bases. The trachea and bronchi have abnormal course and caliber. The great vessels have a normal course, caliber and standard branching pattern. The heart has normal size and appearance. There is no pericardial effusion. Evaluation of the lungs demonstrates innumerable subcentimeter pulmonary nodules bilaterally, right greater than left. There is right lymphangitic carcinomatosis, with a small, nodular pleural fluid collection, and diffuse right lung intralobular septal thickening. A chest tube is in place along the right posterior pleural space, extending to the apex. In the right lower lobe, there is a scirrhous mass lesion, which measures 1.8 x 2.1 cm, immediately inferior to the central bronchovascular bundle. This may simply represent lymphangitic spread of metastatic disease or a primary lesion with central necrosis. Additionally, there is a large subcarinal mass which spreads along the airway is measuring approximately 2.7 x 2.1 cm which may represent a large metastatic lymph node or primary mass. There are no focal areas of atelectasis or airspace consolidation. Limited arterial phase evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. Evaluation of the bones demonstrates few patchy sclerotic areas, in the body of the sternum (series 3, image 137), as well as within the body of the T5 vertebra (series 3, image 136), consistent with the osseous metastases. Bone scintigraphy is more sensitive for evaluation of osseous metastases. IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. EXTENSIVE METASTATIC CARCINOMA, WITH LYMPHANGITIC CARCINOMATOSIS, RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT AND INNUMERABLE SUBCENTIMETER PULMONARY NODULES BILATERALLY. 3.2. 7 X 2.1 CM SUBCARINAL MASS LESION AS DESCRIBED ABOVE. ADDITIONAL 1.8 X 2.1 RIGHT LOWER LOBE SCIRRHOUS MASS, MAY REPRESENT LYMPHANGITIC SPREAD ALONG THE BRONCHOVASCULAR BUNDLE OR PRIMARY MASS LESION WITH NECROSIS. 4. SCLEROTIC FOCI WITHIN THE BODY OF THE STERNUM AND T5 VERTEBRAL BODY COMPATIBLE WITH OSSEOUS METASTASES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: CHEST: The great vessels are normal in appearance without evidence for aortic injury. Heart size is normal without pericardial effusion. Calcification is present within the LAD (image 2/183). The tracheobronchial tree is patent. Esophagus is collapsed. 4 mm hypoattenuating nodules are in the right thyroid lobe. Prominent lymph nodes are in the prevascular space, though not pathologically enlarged. There is no hilar or axillary adenopathy. Dependent atelectasis is present at the lung bases. There is no pneumothorax. No pulmonary nodules or masses are present. Hardware related to anterior spinal fusion is partially included at the lower cervical spine. Degenerative changes are present in the lower cervical spine with irregularity at the anterior aspect of the superior endplate of C7 (image 103/87). There is no evidence of sternal fracture or underlying hematoma. ABDOMEN: The liver is normal in morphology and attenuation. There is no biliary ductal dilatation. Gallbladder appears normal. The spleen is normal in appearance without perisplenic fluid. The pancreas is somewhat atrophic. Adrenal glands and kidneys are normal in appearance. There is no evidence of small bowel dilatation. Colon is grossly unremarkable. A normal appendix is visualized. There is no free intraperitoneal air and no free fluid within the mesentery. PELVIS: Bladder, uterus, and ovaries are grossly unremarkable. There is no free fluid in the pelvis. No deep pelvic or inguinal adenopathy is present. There is mild anterior wedging of the L1 vertebral body with irregularity of the anterior cortex, adjacent to the superior endplate. A horizontal sclerotic line within the vertebral body extends towards this defect. There is no spondylolisthesis. Mild levoscoliosis of the lumbar spine is noted, as well as sacralization of the right L5 hemivertebra. Sclerotic density in the right iliac wing could represent a large bone island, as may sclerotic focus in the right sacral ala. Increased density within the subcutaneous soft tissue posterior to the lumbar spine may represent hematoma in this region. IMPRESSION: 1. PROBABLE COMPRESSION FRACTURE OF L1 WITH LESS THAN 25% LOSS OF VERTEBRAL BODY HEIGHT. 2. DEGENERATIVE CHANGES LIKELY ACCOUNT FOR IRREGULARITY IN THE LOWER CERVICAL SPINE, BUT EVALUATION FOR CLINICAL SYMPTOMS REFERABLE TO THIS AREA SHOULD BE PERFORMED TO EXCLUDE TRAUMATIC INJURY. 3. PRELIMINARY RESULTS WERE PROVIDED TO THE ER RESIDENT AT 1734 HOURS. ON DISCUSSION WITH THE TRAUMA RESIDENT, skinner elliana j dr., BY THE RADIOLOGY FELLOW ON THE FOLLOWING DAY, THEY WERE AWARE OF PROBABLE L1 FRACTURE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: anterior aspect of superior endplate of C7 (location:present)
subcutaneous soft tissue posterior to lumbar spine (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: Scattered subcentimeter nonspecific low-density intrahepatic lesions. Mild dilatation of the imaged intrahepatic biliary tree, a nonspecific finding. The extrahepatic biliary system is non-visualized. Imaged portions of the abdomen are otherwise grossly unremarkable. Normal heart size. No pericardial effusion. No pulmonary intra-arterial filling defects. No intrathoracic adenopathy. Several small prevascular and subcarinal lymph nodes are incidentally noted. No pericardial or pleural effusion. Moderate pulmonary emphysema with bibasilar dorsal atelectatic changes. Patent central tracheobronchial tree. No airspace consolidation. Fine subpleural lines and intralobular reticular opacities could reflect mild underlying pulmonary fibrotic changes or manifestations of atelectasis. Several nonspecific ground-glass nodular opacities are noted within the left upper lobe, presumably inflammatory. Intrabronchial fluid / debris identified within several right lower lobe segmental and subsegmental airways (particularly the lateral and posterior basilar segments) with corresponding peribronchial bandlike parenchymal opacities, possibly manifestations of atelectasis. There are degenerative changes of the spine. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. BRONCHIAL WALL THICKENING AND SCATTERED PERIBRONCHIOLAR GROUND-GLASS NODULAR OPACITIES, SUGGESTIVE OF SMOKING-RELATED INFLAMMATORY AIRWAYS DISEASE. NO AIRSPACE CONSOLIDATION. 3. RIGHT LOWER LOBE SEGMENTAL AND SUBSEGMENTAL INTRABRONCHIAL FLUID, POSSIBLY INSPISSATED AIRWAY SECRETIONS OR ASPIRATED DEBRIS. 4. NONSPECIFIC INTRAHEPATIC BILIARY DUCTAL DILATATION, INCOMPLETELY EVALUATED. IF ADDITIONAL IMAGING IS NEEDED, ULTRASOUND MAY PROVE USEFUL FOR FURTHER ASSESSMENT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Thyroid: Imaged portions of the thyroid gland are unremarkable. Arch: There is atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic aorta, which is normal in caliber and demonstrates standard 3-vessel arch anatomy. Heart: The right heart is mildly enlarged. There is no pericardial effusion. A cardiac pacemaker is seen in the left chest wall with leads in the right atrial appendage and the right ventricle. There are coronary artery calcifications. Pulmonary Vasculature: There is a linear filling defect in a segmental branch of the right lower lobe pulmonary artery best seen on series 2 image # 158. This appears adherent to the vessel wall and is not located at a bifurcation. C This is consistent with an age indeterminant but likely chronic pulmonary embolus. The remainder of the pulmonary arteries are clear. Lungs / Pleura: There are advanced centrilobular and paraseptal emphysematous changes. Scattered calcified granulomas are noted. There is a 4-mm right upper lobe pulmonary nodule, best seen on series 2 image # 293, with apparent central cavitation. A smaller right upper lobe nodule seen on image # 268. There is marked peribronchial thickening with areas of lower lobe mucus plugging and associated patchy air space consolidation. Mediastinum: There are no pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes by size criteria. Hila: There are calcified hilar lymph nodes and. Axillae: Clear. Upper abdomen: Calcified granulomas are noted in the spleen. There is reflux of contrast into the IVC and hepatic veins, suggesting cardiac dysfunction. Skeletal structures: No destructive osseous lesions are seen. IMPRESSION: 1. AGE INDETERMINANT BUT CHRONIC APPEARING PULMONARY EMBOLUS IN A SEGMENTAL BRANCH OF THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE PULMONARY ARTERY. 2. ADVANCED EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGES. THERE IS MARKED PERIBRONCHIAL THICKENING AND MUCOUS PLUGGING, AND THE APPEARANCE IS MOST TYPICAL FOR BRONCHITIS. 3. THERE IS A 4-MM RIGHT UPPER LOBE PULMONARY NODULE, WITH APPARENT CENTRAL CAVITATION. CORRELATION WITH ANY PRIOR OUTSIDE IMAGING STUDIES IS RECOMMENDED. ALTERNATIVELY, 3-6 MONTH FOLLOW-UP IS RECOMMENDED TO DOCUMENT STABILITY. 4. MILD CARDIOMEGALY. Preliminary findings were reported on 8-22-19 at 21:17. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: segmental branch of right lower lobe pulmonary artery (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: The soft tissues of the lower neck and thyroid are unremarkable. No axillary, supraclavicular, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy. The trachea and central airways have normal course, caliber, and standard branching pattern. The great vessels have normal caliber and standard branching pattern. The aortic arch has 3 vessel branching anatomy. The heart has normal size and appearance. No pericardial effusion. Minimal calcification is seen in the left anterior descending artery. No pulmonary embolism is identified. Compared with prior studies in 8/13/2017 and 8/13/2012, there are multiple small ground glass nodules, measuring up to 5 mm. These can be identified on the following images: Right upper lobe lesions: series 2, images 236, 264, 290, 309, and 318. The right lower lobe: Series 2 images 151, 175, 213, 227, 236, and 245. Right middle: No ground glass opacities. Left upper lobe: Series 2, image 309, 294 and 264. Left lower lobe: No ground glass opacities. No pleural effusions. Arterial phase imaging of the upper abdomen demonstrates mild reflux of contrast into the hepatic veins, but is otherwise unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM IDENTIFIED. 2. SCATTERED FEW GROUND GLASS OPACITIES IN THE BILATERAL UPPER LOBES ARE UNCHANGED FROM 8/13/2017, AND SEEN PREVIOUSLY FROM 8-13-2012. THESE MAY REPRESENT ATYPICAL ADENOMATOUS HYPERPLASIA. CONSIDER CONTINUED FOLLOW UP, IF CLINICALLY APPROPRIATE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

input: FINDINGS: Evaluation of the pulmonary arteries demonstrates filling defects in the right upper lobar and segmental arteries, compatible with acute pulmonary emboli. Tiny left lower lobe segmental eccentric webs are seen, which could represent subacute or chronic emboli. Main pulmonary artery and right heart appear normal in size. Evaluation of the heart demonstrates normal heart size. Small pericardial effusion is again seen, although on the prior study the fluid was in a more posterior location. Three vessel coronary artery calcification is seen. Aortic valve is partially calcified. No evidence of dissection. 3-vessel arch is noted. Evaluation of the axilla, mediastinum, and hilum demonstrates no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Redemonstration of gastric pull-through. Evaluation of the lungs demonstrates severe emphysema and resolved pleural effusions. Right upper lobe posterior opacities are seen, which could represent atelectasis. A small cluster of centrilobular nodules is seen in the right lower lobe, not clearly seen on the prior exam although significant opacification previously limited evaluation. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen in pulmonary arterial contrast phase demonstrates extensive symmetric bilateral perinephric straining, similar in appearance to the prior exam 2-7-2008. Evaluation of the visualized osseous structures demonstrates no suspicious appearing lesions. Chronic appearing calcifications is seen along the right fifth and sixth ribs, which could be related to old trauma. Multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. IMPRESSION: 1. RIGHT UPPER LOBAR AND SEGMENTAL ACUTE PULMONARY EMBOLI. LEFT LOWER LOBE SEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLI, WHICH COULD BE SUBACUTE OR CHRONIC. 2. RIGHT LOWER LOBE CLUSTER OF CENTRILOBULAR NODULES, WHICH COULD BE INFECTIOUS OR INFLAMMATORY IN NATURE. INTERVAL RESOLUTION OF PREVIOUSLY SEEN PLEURAL EFFUSIONS. 3. EXTENSIVE SYMMETRIC PERINEPHRIC STRANDING, SIMILAR IN APPEARANCE TO PRIOR EXAM. 4. REDEMONSTRATION OF GASTRIC PULL THROUGH. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The exam is markedly limited by respiratory motion artifact. The left lobe of the thyroid gland appears somewhat heterogeneous, and is incompletely evaluated due to non-contiguous technique. Multiple prominent mediastinal lymph nodes are identified measuring up to 1.2 cm in diameter, grossly stable compared to the prior exam. The pulmonary artery is at the upper limits of normal for size, measuring approximately 29 mm in diameter. The heart is normal in size, without evidence of pericardial effusion. Marked calcific atherosclerotic changes are identified in the visualized portions of the coronary arteries. There is no evidence of pleural effusion. Evaluation of the lung demonstrates diffuse centrilobular nodularity with tree-in-bud appearance, increased compared to the prior exam. Diffuse bronchiectasis and bronchial wall thickening is again identified bilaterally, most marked in the right middle lobe, lingula, and bilateral lower lobes. Surgical sutures are identified within the right middle lobe, compatible with prior biopsy. Evaluation of the upper abdominal structures is limited by non-contiguous technique and lack of intravenous contrast material. Scattered calcific atherosclerotic changes of the aorta and mesenteric vessels are identified. Otherwise, the visualized portions of the upper abdomen are unremarkable. Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are identified. No bony destructive lesions are demonstrated. IMPRESSIONS: 1. INTERVAL INCREASE IN BRONCHIECTASIS, BRONCHIAL WALL THICKENING, AND CENTRILOBULAR NODULARITY COMPARED TO PRIOR EXAM, COMPATIBLE WITH INTERVAL WORSENING OF ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC BRONCHIOLITIS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: HEART / VASCULATURE: The heart is normal in size, without pericardial effusion. There are several foci of fat within the right ventricular wall. Two left pulmonary veins and two right pulmonary veins drain into the left atrium without obstruction. There is no evidence of thrombus within the left atrium or left atrial appendage. The right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, left anterior descending coronary artery, and left circumflex coronary artery demonstrate mild to moderate atherosclerotic calcification, without significant stenosis. Note is made of a ramus intermedius coronary artery. The patient is right coronary artery dominant. The thoracic aorta is normal in course and caliber. The great vessels are unremarkable. No obvious intraluminal filling defects are seen within the pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. The pulmonary artery are normal in course and caliber. OTHER FINDINGS: The thyroid gland appears normal. The central airways are patent. No pathologically enlarged axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymph nodes are identified. There is subsegmental atelectasis versus scarring within both lobes. The visualized lungs are otherwise clear. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates mild symmetric bilateral perinephric stranding. There is lobulation of the anterior upper pole of the right kidney. There is also a 7 mm cyst in the upper pole of the right kidney. Note is made of an accessory right hepatic artery arising from the superior mesenteric artery. There is no acute osseous abnormality. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: # czfloguiiat Diameters in mm Major Minor Right superior pulmonary vein origin 25.50 23.90 Right inferior pulmonary vein origin 26.00 23.40 Left superior pulmonary vein origin 30.20 13.90 Left inferior pulmonary vein origin 19.90 14.80 Areas: (in mm2) Area Right superior pulmonary vein origin-area 498.80 Right inferior pulmonary vein origin-area 491.00 Left superior pulmonary vein origin-area 323.10 Left inferior pulmonary vein origin-area 226.30 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends Addendum Begins Addendum: HISTORY: 68-year-old male with history of atrial fibrillation. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. TWO RIGHT AND TWO LEFT PULMONARY VEINS DRAIN UNOBSTRUCTED INTO THE LEFT ATRIUM. 2. MODERATE MULTIVESSEL CORONARY ARTERIAL CALCIFICATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Contrast opacifies the bilateral pulmonary arteries and segmental branches with no pulmonary embolism. There is mild narrowing of subsegmental left lower lobe pulmonary artery branches from a left lower lobe mass described below. There is extensive subcutaneous and deep soft tissue nodularity throughout the visualized chest and upper abdomen. This extensive soft tissue disease extends into the bilateral axillae, mediastinum, and supraclavicular regions with superior extension and displacement of the great vessels of the neck anteriorly. The brachiocephalic, visualized left carotid and left subclavian arteries are patent. There is also extensive subpleural nodularity involving both lungs. There are multiple pulmonary nodules; the largest representative areas include a right upper lobe lobulated nodule measuring 19 x 15 mm compared to 13 x 13 mm on prior examination. A second dominant mass within the more central left lower lobe measures 3.8 x 4.5 cm compared to 2.4 x 2.9 cm on prior examination. The dominant subpleural mass is at the left apex and measures 3.4 x 4.5 cm compared to 2.0 x 2.6 cm on prior examination. There are new small, right greater than left, pleural effusions without discrete pleural enhancement but with some areas of nodularity. There are scattered, ill-defined ground-glass opacities within the lungs, predominately on the right. UPPER ABDOMEN: The visualized portions of the upper abdomen demonstrate extensive soft tissue nodularity as well as mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, inadequately visualized on current phase of imaging. The left suprarenal space is diffusely involved by a large mass measuring approximately 7 x 3.5 cm and a separate left adrenal gland is not visualized. There is extensive nodularity within the right perinephric region as well, all of which has progressed compared to the 2008 PET CT. SKELETAL: No discrete osseous disease. No fractures. IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. EXTENSIVE SOFT TISSUE METASTASES WITHIN THE VISUALIZED DEEP AND SUBCUTANEOUS SOFT TISSUES OF THE CHEST AND UPPER ABDOMEN WITH SIGNIFICANT INVOLVEMENT OF THE MEDIASTINUM, PLEURA, AND PULMONARY PARENCHYMA INDICATING SIGNIFICANT DISEASE PROGRESSION COMPARED TO OUTSIDE PET CT 2000 MAY 11. SMALL, RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT, PLEURAL EFFUSIONS. PRELIMINARY READING WAS GIVEN AT TIME OF EXAMINATION ON february 19th, 2001. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

related: subsegmental left lower lobe pulmonary artery branches (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: The main pulmonary artery measures 3.2 cm in diameter, compatible with underlying pulmonary arterial hypertension. The pericardium is within normal limits. Subcentimeter axillary and mediastinal nodes are identified. Evaluation for hilar nodes is limited due to noncontiguous imaging and lack of intravenous contrast. The central airways are patent. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is identified. Respiratory motion slightly limits evaluation. There is no reticulation, architectural distortion, traction bronchiectasis, or honeycomb to suggest interstitial lung disease. A 0.9 cm lucency along the right major fissure (series 2, image 24) may represent a bleb or small, loculated pneumothorax. Additional scattered lucencies are identified, probably related to emphysematous changes. Additional more irregular lucencies are identified in the posterior right costophrenic angle, intermixed with ground glass opacities. It is unclear whether these latter lucencies are related to pneumatoceles, whether from prior infection or trauma. The ground glass opacities are dependent and do not resolve in the prone position. The gallbladder is nonvisualized. The spleen is large. No significant osseous abnormalities are identified. IMPRESSION: 1. NO FINDINGS TO SUGGEST INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE. 2. PATCHY GROUND GLASS OPACITIES IN THE POSTERIOR RIGHT COSTOPHRENIC ANGLE, OF UNCLEAR CHRONICITY. INFECTION IS CONSIDERED IN THE ACUTE SETTING. 3. SCATTERED FOCAL LUCENCIES WHICH ARE NONSPECIFIC BUT COULD REPRESENT EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGES AND / OR PNEUMATOCELES FROM PRIOR INFECTION / TRAUMA. 4. LARGE MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY COMPATIBLE WITH UNDERLYING PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION. 5. SUBMISSION OF ANY AVAILABLE PRIOR EXAMS WOULD BE HELPFUL FOR COMPARISON. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is a segmental filling defect within the medial segment pulmonary artery branch to the right middle lobe, consistent with pulmonary embolism. No other filling defects to suggest pulmonary embolism are identified. However, motion limits the detection of small peripheral pulmonary emboli on this examination, particularly in the left lower lobe. These findings were discussed with dr. jessica a on 6-12 at approximately 1200 hours. The heart appears enlarged. There is nasogastric tube present, with tip in the central portion of the duodenum. The thoracic vasculature is unremarkable. No abnormal mediastinal, hilar, or axillary adenopathy is identified. There is a new small area of consolidation within the left lung base, which may represent infection, aspiration, and / or atelectasis. Correlate clinically. Dependent changes are noted bilaterally. The lungs are otherwise clear with no evidence of pneumothorax or pleural effusion. There are two approximately 1 cm hypodense liver lesions near the liver dome and within the left liver lobe, which may have some associated hyperemia. These are too small to characterize. They are stable since May 10th, 2004, but new since 9/9 / 1998. Therefore, continued attention on followup imaging is recommended. The liver is otherwise unremarkable. The gallbladder, spleen, adrenal glands, pancreas, kidneys, and visualized vasculature are unremarkable. There is a Foley catheter present within a decompressed urinary bladder, which is otherwise not well evaluated. No new suspicious abdominal or pelvic adenopathy is identified. There has been interval, further decrease in size of several predominantly upper abdominal abscesses. One still definable abscess within the left lower quadrant measures approximately 10 mm, compared to 15 mm on the prior examination. The remainder of the small described abscesses are no longer visualized, many of which have been replaced with phlegmonous changes. Right upper quadrant inflammatory changes persist and presumably related to laparotomy. There has been a mild interval increase in the dilation of numerous small bowel loops consistent with an early or partial small bowel obstruction. A transition point is identified on axial 457 of series 3, near midline. Distal to the transition point, the bowel dilates, but then strictures once again, and an additional second transition point is not completely excluded. The distal small bowel is collapsed. The overall appearance of the large bowel is stable, including a featureless sigmoid. No new focal fluid collections are identified. There is no free air. There is a left-sided PICC line visualized which forms a loop near the junction of the subclavian and brachiocephalic veins, then terminates near the cavoatrial junction. No new suspicious focal osseous lesion is identified. IMPRESSION: 1. SEGMENTAL RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE PULMONARY EMBOLISM. THIS WAS DISCUSSED WITH dr. berro AT APPROXIMATELY 1200 HOURS ON 6/12/2004. EVALUATION OF SMALLER PERIPHERAL EMBOLI IS LIMITED DUE TO RESPIRATORY MOTION, PARTICULARLY WITHIN THE LEFT LOWER LOBE. 2. SMALL AREA OF LEFT LOWER LOBE CONSOLIDATION MAY REPRESENT ATELECTASIS, ASPIRATION, AND / OR INFECTION. CORRELATE CLINICALLY. 3. CONTINUED INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF INTRAABDOMINAL ABSCESSES, WITH ONLY A SINGLE DEFINABLE 1 CM COLLECTION REMAINING. THE REST REMAIN AS RESIDUAL PHLEMONOUS CHANGES. RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT INFLAMMATORY CHANGES PRESUMABLY RELATED TO LAPAROTOMY. 4. WORSENING EARLY OR PARTIAL SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION AS DESCRIBED, INCLUDING IDENTIFIED TRANSITION POINT WITHIN MID ABDOMEN. IRREGULARITY AND STRICTURING OF SMALL BOWEL DISTAL TO THE IDENTIFIED TRANSITION POINT AND DOWNSTREAM, SECOND STRICTURE IS NOT COMPLETELY EXCLUDED. 5. STABLE APPEARANCE OF COLON, INCLUDING FEATURELESS SIGMOID. 6. TWO HYPODENSE LIVER LESIONS AS DESCRIBED, STABLE SINCE 05/10/2004, BUT NEW SINCE 9/9 / 1998. CONTINUED ATTENTION ON FOLLOWUP IMAGING IS RECOMMENDED. 7. NASOGASTRIC TUBE TERMINATING WITHIN THE SECOND PORTION OF THE DUODENUM. 8. LEFT-SIDED PICC LINE LOOPING NEAR JUNCTION OF BRACHIOCEPHALIC AND SUBCLAVIAN VEINS, TERMINATING NEAR CAVOATRIAL JUNCTION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: medial segment pulmonary artery branch to right middle lobe (location:present)
junction of subclavian and brachiocephalic veins (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: The study is limited secondary to streak artifact from patient's body habitus. There is suboptimal contrast opacification of the pulmonary arterial tree with attenuation in the main pulmonary artery measuring 142 Hounsfield units. There are no filling defects within the central pulmonary arterial tree to suggest an embolus. There is cardiomegaly but no pericardial effusion. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates small bilateral pleural effusions. Additionally, there are areas of peribronchovascular ground glass opacities and nodularity involving the upper lobes bilaterally and to a lesser extent within the left lower lobe. There is a 1.7-cm right subpectoral lymph node, with small subcentimeter lymph nodes seen in the right axilla and mediastinum but not pathologically enlarged by CT size criteria. Limited evaluation of the visualized upper abdominal viscera is unremarkable. Degenerative changes are seen in the spine. IMPRESSION: 1. SUBOPTIMAL OPACIFICATION OF THE PULMONARY ARTERIAL TREE, HOWEVER NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLUS IN THE CENTRAL PULMONARY ARTERIES. 2. SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH PATCHY GROUND GLASS PARENCHYMAL OPACITIES IN THE BILATERAL UPPER AND LEFT LOWER LOBES CONCERNING FOR MULTIFOCAL PNEUMONIA. 3. CARDIOMEGALY. PRELIMINARY REPORT WAS CREATED BY THE RADIOLOGY RESIDENT ON-CALL ON 2-16-2004 AT 1710 HOURS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: CARDIOVASCULAR: The heart is displaced into the left chest by the enlarging mass within the right hemithorax. Pericardial effusion is noted. There is biatrial compression. Definite involvement by tumor is not assessed on this exam. A left central venous catheter is noted with its tip in the contralateral brachiocephalic vein. There is minimal narrowing of the right brachiocephalic vein at this region without definite evidence of stenosis. The superior vena cava above the stent-graft appears minimally narrowed in AP dimension, however, grossly patent. The inferior vena cava stent extending from the intrahepatic portion, overlaps the superior vena cava stent in the right atrium. The inferior vena cava below the stent-graft is widely patent without evidence of thrombosis. A left PICC line is partially visualized on this exam. CHEST: An enormous, heterogeneous, low-attenuation mass with septation, is noted involving the entire right hemithorax with, areas of fluid attenuation. This mass predominantly involves the pleural space with complete collapse of the right lung, with displacement of the mediastinum into the contralateral chest. The lower portion of this mass has a lobulated appearance, extends behind the heart into the left chest. The effusion in the left chest has increased in the interim with adjacent compressive atelectasis in the lower lobe. Several foci of high attenuation are noted within the pleural space and the right chest adjacent to the diaphragm. A left upper lobe nodule is noted measuring 6 mm on series 4, image 51. The trachea is deviated to the left from the enlarging right chest mass. There is complete compression of the right mainstem bronchus and its branches. BODY: Abdominal as well as pelvic ascites is noted. The liver, kidneys, spleen, pancreas, adrenals and gallbladder appear unremarkable. Colonic diverticulosis is noted without definite evidence of diverticulitis. No osseous abnormality. IMPRESSION: 1. THE SUPERIOR VENA CAVA ABOVE THE GRAFT APPEARS MINIMALLY REDUCED IN AP DIMENSION, HOWEVER, GROSSLY PATENT. THE INFERIOR VENA CAVA BELOW THE STENT IS WIDELY PATENT. GOOD CONTRAST OPACIFICATION WITHIN THE VENOATRIAL STENT-GRAFT WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF THROMBOSIS. NO EVIDENCE OF CENTRAL CLOT. 2. REDEMONSTRATION OF A LARGE RIGHT CHEST MASS OCCUPYING THE ENTIRE RIGHT HEMITHORAX AND A SIGNIFICANT PORTION OF THE LEFT CHEST ACROSS THE MIDLINE. THIS IS SLIGHTLY LARGER WHEN COMPARED TO THE MOST RECENT EXAMINATION FROM 11-02-04. LEFT PLEURAL EFFUSION HAS ALSO INCREASED IN THE INTERIM. 3. ABDOMINAL AS WELL AS PELVIC ASCITES. SOFT TISSUE EDEMA. 4. PERICARDIAL EFFUSION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Respiratory motion slightly limits evaluation of the subsegmental pulmonary arteries. The main, lobar, and segmental pulmonary arteries show no intraluminal filling defect to indicate pulmonary embolism. There is new diffuse ground glass opacity, left lung worse than right lung, with patchy opacities in the left lower lobe. Also seen is mild bronchial wall thickening. Bilateral upper lobe calcified granulomas are redemonstrated. Previously-described pulmonary nodules are obscured by new ground glass opacity and patchy consolidations. Again seen are postsurgical changes of right middle lobe wedge resection. Dependent debris is seen within the central airways. The heart size is enlarged, and there is a small pericardial effusion. Coronary artery stents are present. There is mild calcified atherosclerotic plaque within the thoracic aorta. A left upper chest Mediport enters the left subclavian vein and terminates at the cavoatrial junction. The arch vessels are normal in course and caliber. Postsurgical changes of the lower neck, including thyroidectomy, are redemonstrated. There is interval increase in size of mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes. For instance, a prevascular lymph node is increased to 9 mm in short axis (3/142) from 1-2 mm previously. Numerous aortopulmonary window lymph nodes are also increased in size, measuring up to 10 mm in short axis (3/140). A right hilar lymph node is increased to 13 mm in short axis (3/90) from 10 mm previously. A left hilar lymph node now measures 9 mm in short axis (3/97) compared with 5 mm previously. New small left and trace right pleural effusions are noted. The visualized upper abdomen is unremarkable. No suspicious osseous lesion identified. Stable multilevel degenerative changes of the spine with several mild vertebral body compression deformities. IMPRESSION: 1. RESPIRATORY MOTION SLIGHTLY LIMITS EVALUATION OF THE SUBSEGMENTAL PULMONARY ARTERIES. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM IN THE MAIN, LOBAR, AND SEGMENTAL PULMONARY ARTERIES. 2. OVERALL FINDINGS OF NEW DIFFUSE, LEFT LUNG GREATER THAN RIGHT LUNG, GROUND GLASS OPACITY AND BRONCHIAL WALL THICKENING ARE MOST CONSISTENT WITH ATYPICAL INFECTION. 3. INTERVAL INCREASE IN MEDIASTINAL AND HILAR ADENOPATHY IS NONSPECIFIC AND LIKELY REACTIVE. RECOMMEND ATTENTION ON FOLLOW-UP STUDIES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The visualized thyroid gland is unremarkable. A right internal jugular line terminates at the caval atrial junction. There is no significant axillary, supraclavicular, mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. There are minimal coronary arterial calcifications. The ascending aorta remains normal in course and caliber. There is standard three vessel branching of the great arch vessels. The proximal great vessels are patent and normal in caliber. There is a focal contour deformity involving less than 25% of the circumference of the proximal descending thoracic aorta anteriorly just distal to the ligamentum arteriosum series 5, image 76, compatible with known traumatic aortic injury. Findings are similar in extent and appearance in comparison to prior exam dated 07/10/2002 and are compatible with an injury to the intima and media and associated pseudoaneurysm formation. A small para-aortic hematoma is again noted. The remainder of the descending thoracic aorta is otherwise normal in course and caliber. The main and lobar pulmonary arteries are normal in course and caliber. While not a dedicated pulmonary embolism study, no large filling defects are seen in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. The trachea and central bronchi are patent. There is been interval increase in size of a still small right pneumothorax. No abnormal mediastinal air is seen. Interval development of a moderate, simple right and small, mildly complex left pleural effusions with associated compressive atelectasis at the lung bases. Visualized images of the upper abdomen demonstrate free fluid compatible with known hemoperitoneum.. Evaluation of the visualized osseous structures again demonstrates a stable fractures of the T2 and T3 posterior spinous processes which may not be acute in nature. IMPRESSION: 1. STABLE TRAUMATIC AORTIC INJURY CHARACTERIZED BY AN INJURY TO THE INTIMA AND MEDIA WITH AN ASSOCIATED SMALL PSEUDOANEURYSM INVOLVING THE PROXIMAL DESCENDING AORTA JUST BELOW THE LEVEL OF THE LIGAMENTUM ARTERIOSUM. 2. INTERVAL INCREASE IN SIZE OF A STILL SMALL RIGHT PNEUMOTHORAX WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF TENSION. 3. INTERVAL INCREASE IN SIZE OF COMPLEX LEFT AND SIMPLE RIGHT PLEURAL FLUID COLLECTIONS. 4. HEMOPERITONEUM. 5. NO ABNORMAL MEDIASTINAL AIR IS SEEN. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Large layering right pleural effusion associate with near complete atelectatic collapse of the right lower lobe. Extensive breathing motion artifact throughout. Exclusion from the field of view of the most inferior left posterior lung base. Pulmonary arteries are without endoluminal filling defect to suggest embolism. The main pulmonary artery is of normal caliber and contour throughout. No endoluminal lesions are evident within the central airways. The trachea and central airways are patent The mediastinum and hila demonstrate no significantly enlarged lymph nodes; and, the bilateral axillary lymph nodes are not pathologically enlarged. A prominence and 7.5-mm paratracheal node is noted. The cardiac chambers and pericardium are within normal limits. The chest wall soft tissues and osseous structures are within normal limits. Limited views of the abdominal cavity demonstrate unchanged appearance of cirrhotic liver morphology and ascites. Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM EVIDENT. 2. LARGE RIGHT LAYERING PLEURAL EFFUSION ASSOCIATED WITH NEAR COMPLETE COLLAPSE OF THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE. 3. REDEMONSTRATION OF ASCITES AND CIRRHOTIC LIVER MORPHOLOGY. Note: Findings discussed with dr. carson after exam completion. QA: QAT SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: A tunneled right internal jugular central venous catheter is again noted, with interval decrease in adjacent subcutaneous gas in the right anterior chest wall. Evaluation of the pulmonary arteries demonstrates two tiny subsegmental filling defects within the left lower lobe (series 2, image 317) and left upper lobe (series 2, image 374). The main pulmonary artery is enlarged, measuring up to 36 mm in diameter. Evaluation of the heart demonstrates an enlarged right ventricle. No pericardial effusions. Coronary arteries again show calcification. Evaluation of the axilla, mediastinum, and hilum demonstrates no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates interval increase in bibasilar consolidation, right greater than left. Redemonstration of postsurgical changes related to multiple prior right-sided wedge resections. Multiple scattered bilateral pulmonary nodules are again noted, some of which are not as well seen due to the interval increase in consolidation, however not significantly changed relative to the recent prior study. A moderate right pleural effusion persists. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen in pulmonary arterial contrast phase again demonstrates multiple ill-defined hypoattenuating lesions throughout the liver, incompletely evaluated on this phase of imaging. Slight interval increase in small amount of visualized abdominal free fluid. Evaluation of the osseous structures demonstrates several mixed lytic and sclerotic foci throughout the ribs, including within the posterior right fourth rib (series 2, image 395), the lateral right sixth rib (series 2, image 294), and the left posterior fifth rib (series 2, image 357). These do not appear significantly changed relative to the prior studies. No new suspicious foci are identified.. IMPRESSION: 1. TWO TINY SUBSEGMENTAL FILLING DEFECTS WITHIN THE LEFT LOWER AND LEFT UPPER LOBE, AS DESCRIBED, UNLIKELY TO ACCOUNT FOR HEART STRAIN REPORTED ON ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY. 2. ENLARGEMENT OF THE MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY AS MAY BE SEEN IN THE CONTEXT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION. 3. SLIGHT INTERVAL INCREASE IN BIBASILAR CONSOLIDATION, RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT, WHICH PARTIALLY OBSCURES KNOWN BILATERAL PULMONARY NODULES. 4. The aortic arch is normal in appearance, without evidence of mediastinal hematoma or traumatic aortic injury. The ascending and descending thoracic aortas are normal. Heart size is normal. There are no enlarged mediastinal, axillary, or hilar lymph nodes. There is no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. With the exception of minimal atelectasis, both lungs are clear. There is no focal consolidation to suggest pneumonia. No nodule or mass is identified. The subcutaneous soft tissues of the chest are normal, without evidence of contusion or injury. ABDOMEN / PELVIS: The liver is normal in appearance, without evidence of focal liver lesion or traumatic injury. There is no perihepatic hematoma. There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation, with the common bile duct at the level of the pancreatic head measuring 3 mm. The gallbladder is normal in appearance, without evidence of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. The spleen is normal in appearance, without evidence of perisplenic hematoma or splenic laceration. The pancreas is normal in appearance, without evidence of peripancreatic free fluid, inflammation, pancreatic mass, or ductal dilatation. Both adrenal glands are normal, without evidence of a nodule or mass. Both kidneys are normal, without evidence of hydronephrosis or stone. The intrahepatic IVC and hepatic veins are patent. The portal veins are patent and normal in size. The splenic vein and SMV are patent. The stomach and duodenum are in normal anatomic positions. Small bowel loops are normal in caliber, without evidence of dilatation or wall thickening. The colon is filled with a mild amount of stool, but is otherwise normal in appearance, without evidence of diverticulosis or diverticulitis. The terminal ileum and appendix are both normal in the right lower quadrant (4/90). There is no free fluid, loculated fluid collection, or abscess. There are no enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen or pelvis. VASCULATURE: The thoracic and abdominal aorta are normal in caliber, with patent branch vessels. There is no significant vascular stenosis. There is no perivascular hematoma to suggest traumatic vascular injury. BONES: Spinal alignment is normal, without evidence of fracture. No acute fractures are identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. There are no suspicious lytic or sclerotic bone lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC INJURY TO THE CHEST. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC AORTIC INJURY. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC INJURY TO THE ABDOMEN OR PELVIS. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.

SINGLE FRONTAL RADIOGRAPH OF THE CHEST DEMONSTRATES EXCLUDES THE BILATERAL LUNG BASES. UNCHANGED APPEARANCE OF RIGHT UPPER EXTREMITY PICC LINE, TRACHEOSTOMY CANNULA, ATRIAL APPENDAGE CLIP, AND CARDIAC PACEMAKER / AICD WITH RIGHT ATRIAL LEAD POINTING IN DOWNWARD ORIENTATION.. 2. LUNGS DEMONSTRATE PERSISTENT MILD PULMONARY EDEMA. SMALL LEFT PLEURAL EFFUSION. NO PNEUMOTHORAX. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above. ACCESSION NUMBER: 30_82_66_59 This report has been anonymized. All dates are offset from the actual dates by a fixed interval associated with the patient.

ENTITY FIELDS

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
